questions.question: How does UE Cast<T> work without RTTI?
options: UHT generates StaticClass() with class flags. Cast<T> compares flags — O(1), no vtable walk.
questions.correctAnswer: UHT generates StaticClass() with class flags. Cast<T> compares flags — O(1), no vtable walk.
questions.explanation: DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS generates TCastFlags<T>. Cast<T> checks UObject::GetClass()->HasAnyCastFlag(TCastFlags<T>). Fast, no RTTI.

questions.question: What does Cast<T> return on failure?
options: nullptr — always check result. Cast<T>(Obj) returns nullptr if Obj is not a T.
questions.correctAnswer: nullptr — always check result. Cast<T>(Obj) returns nullptr if Obj is not a T.
questions.explanation: Cast<T> returns T* or nullptr. Pattern: if(auto* Actor = Cast<AActor>(Obj)) { Actor->DoThing(); }. No exception path.

questions.q: What is the UE Cast<T> return value on failure?
o: nullptr — always check return. Cast<T>(Obj) returns T* or nullptr. No exceptions in UE.
questions.a: nullptr — always check return. Cast<T>(Obj) returns T* or nullptr. No exceptions in UE.
questions.e: if(AActor* A = Cast<AActor>(Obj)) { A->DoThing(); }. Null-check pattern. No RTTI, no exception. Fast O(1) class flag comparison.

questions.q: What is the difference between Cast and CastChecked?
o: Cast: returns nullptr on failure. CastChecked: asserts/crashes on failure — use when the type MUST be correct
questions.a: Cast: returns nullptr on failure. CastChecked: asserts/crashes on failure — use when the type MUST be correct
questions.e: CastChecked = 'I know this is the right type.' Crash if wrong (better than silent nullptr bug). Use in controlled paths where failure = logic error.

questions.q: How does Cast<T> achieve O(1) without RTTI?
o: UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags. Cast compares class flags — integer comparison, no vtable walk.
questions.a: UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags. Cast compares class flags — integer comparison, no vtable walk.
questions.e: TCastFlags<T> = compile-time bitmask. GetClass()->HasAnyCastFlag(Flags). One integer AND. Much faster than dynamic_cast (vtable walk + type_info compare).

questions.question: What is the two-phase collision detection architecture?
options: Broad phase (cheap, find potential pairs) + Narrow phase (expensive, precise test on candidates)
questions.correctAnswer: Broad phase (cheap, find potential pairs) + Narrow phase (expensive, precise test on candidates)
questions.explanation: Broad phase: O(n log n) spatial partitioning (grid, octree, sweep-and-prune). Narrow phase: geometric tests (AABB, SAT, GJK).

questions.question: What is the tunneling problem?
options: Fast-moving object passes through thin wall in one frame — collision missed between discrete checks
questions.correctAnswer: Fast-moving object passes through thin wall in one frame — collision missed between discrete checks
questions.explanation: Solution: CCD (Continuous Collision Detection) — sweep shape along path. Or sub-stepping (run physics at higher frequency). UE: bUseCCD.

questions.q: What is the sweep-and-prune broad phase?
o: Sort AABB min-x values, sweep across. Objects with overlapping x-intervals are candidates. Extend to y, z.
questions.a: Sort AABB min-x values, sweep across. Objects with overlapping x-intervals are candidates. Extend to y, z.
questions.e: Sweep-and-prune (sort and sweep): O(n log n) sort, then linear sweep. Good for many moving objects. Used in Bullet Physics.

questions.q: What is the difference between discrete and continuous collision detection?
o: Discrete: checks positions at frame boundaries (can miss fast objects). CCD: sweeps shape along path, catches tunneling.
questions.a: Discrete: checks positions at frame boundaries (can miss fast objects). CCD: sweeps shape along path, catches tunneling.
questions.e: Discrete = snapshot at t=n and t=n+1. CCD = swept volume between. Bullet traveling 1000m/s moves 41m per frame — discrete misses walls.

questions.q: What spatial partitioning structures are used for broad phase?
questions.correctAnswer: Uniform grids, quadtrees/octrees, dynamic AABB trees (DBVT), and sweep-and-prune
acceptableAnswers: grids, octrees, DBVT, sweep and prune
uniform grid, quadtree, octree, DBVT, sort and sweep
questions.explanation: Broad phase reduces O(n²) to O(n log n). Grid: many similar-sized objects. Octree: uneven distribution. DBVT: dynamic worlds (UE uses this).

questions.q: What is a dynamic AABB tree (DBVT)?
o: Binary tree of AABBs — nodes can move incrementally updated. UE's internal broad phase structure.
questions.a: Binary tree of AABBs — nodes can move incrementally updated. UE's internal broad phase structure.
questions.e: DBVT = dynamic bounding volume tree. Leaves are object AABBs. Rotated/translated nodes update cheaply. UE5 Chaos physics uses this.

questions.question: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
options: Concurrency = dealing with multiple things at once (structure); parallelism = doing multiple things at once (execution)
questions.correctAnswer: Concurrency = dealing with multiple things at once (structure); parallelism = doing multiple things at once (execution)
questions.explanation: Concurrency is about program structure. Parallelism is about simultaneous execution on multiple cores.

questions.question: What is a race condition?
options: A threading bug where the result depends on the timing of thread execution order
questions.correctAnswer: A threading bug where the result depends on the timing of thread execution order
questions.explanation: Race condition = output depends on non-deterministic thread interleaving. Undefined behavior in C++.

questions.question: What is the fundamental unit of concurrency in C++?
questions.correctAnswer: std::thread
questions.explanation: std::thread is the C++ standard's threading primitive. Each thread has its own stack and runs independently.

questions.question: What is a mutex?
options: Mutual exclusion — ensures only one thread accesses a critical section at a time
A type of lock-free data structure
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion — ensures only one thread accesses a critical section at a time
questions.explanation: mutex = MUTual EXclusion. lock() blocks until the mutex is available. Only one thread holds it at a time.

questions.question: What problem does std::lock_guard solve?
options: RAII for mutexes — automatically unlocks when going out of scope, even with exceptions
questions.correctAnswer: RAII for mutexes — automatically unlocks when going out of scope, even with exceptions
questions.explanation: lock_guard locks in constructor, unlocks in destructor. Exception-safe: destructor always runs.

questions.question: What is interpolation?
options: Finding intermediate values between two known data points — filling in the gaps between keyframes
questions.correctAnswer: Finding intermediate values between two known data points — filling in the gaps between keyframes
questions.explanation: Interpolation reconstructs continuous motion from discrete samples. Every frame of animation is interpolated between keyframes.

questions.question: What is the difference between lerp and easing functions?
options: Lerp = linear (constant speed). Easing = reshapes t before lerp to create acceleration/deceleration (more natural motion).
questions.correctAnswer: Lerp = linear (constant speed). Easing = reshapes t before lerp to create acceleration/deceleration (more natural motion).
questions.explanation: Lerp: A + (B-A)·t (straight line). Easing: apply function f(t) first → Lerp(A, B, f(t)). t²=ease-in, 1-(1-t)²=ease-out.

questions.question: What is Inverse Kinematics (IK)?
options: Given end effector position, solve for joint angles — 'put the hand HERE, figure out elbow and shoulder'
questions.correctAnswer: Given end effector position, solve for joint angles — 'put the hand HERE, figure out elbow and shoulder'
questions.explanation: IK: target-driven. FK: joint-driven. CCD and FABRIK are two IK algorithms. Used for foot placement, hand reaching, look-at.

questions.q: What is a spline?
o: A piecewise curve made of multiple polynomial segments joined smoothly — like a flexible ruler bent through control points
questions.a: A piecewise curve made of multiple polynomial segments joined smoothly — like a flexible ruler bent through control points
questions.e: Spline = multiple curve pieces with continuity constraints at joints. C0=connected, C1=smooth tangent, C2=smooth curvature.

questions.q: What is arc-length parameterization?
o: Re-parameterizing a curve so equal steps in t produce equal distance traveled — fixes speed non-uniformity
questions.a: Re-parameterizing a curve so equal steps in t produce equal distance traveled — fixes speed non-uniformity
questions.e: Most curves: constant Δt ≠ constant distance. Arc-length: precompute cumulative length table, invert to get t(s). Constant speed motion.

questions.question: What does the call instruction do?
options: Pushes return address (RIP_next) onto stack and jumps to function address
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes return address (RIP_next) onto stack and jumps to function address
questions.explanation: call = push return address + jmp. ret = pop RIP. Pure hardware — no OS, no heap, no allocation.

questions.question: What register points to the top of the stack?
questions.correctAnswer: RSP (Stack Pointer)
questions.explanation: RSP points to the current stack top (lowest active address). push decrements RSP; pop increments it. Stack grows downward.

questions.question: What is a stack frame?
options: The region of stack used by one function call — contains return address, saved RBP, local variables
questions.correctAnswer: The region of stack used by one function call — contains return address, saved RBP, local variables
questions.explanation: Prologue creates frame (push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N). Epilogue destroys it (mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret). RBP = fixed anchor.

questions.question: What is the System V AMD64 calling convention?
options: Linux/macOS ABI: first 6 integer args in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Return in RAX. Red zone below RSP.
questions.correctAnswer: Linux/macOS ABI: first 6 integer args in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Return in RAX. Red zone below RSP.
questions.explanation: System V: 6 register args, red zone (128 bytes below RSP usable without adjustment). Microsoft x64: 4 register args, shadow space.

questions.question: What is the difference between caller-saved and callee-saved registers?
options: Caller-saved: callee can clobber freely (RAX, RCX, RDX, R8-R11). Callee-saved: must preserve (RBX, RBP, R12-R15).
questions.correctAnswer: Caller-saved: callee can clobber freely (RAX, RCX, RDX, R8-R11). Callee-saved: must preserve (RBX, RBP, R12-R15).
questions.explanation: Caller-saved = 'if you need this value after the call, save it yourself.' Callee-saved = 'I promise not to change this.'

questions.question: What does Geometric Algebra unify?
options: Vectors, complex numbers, quaternions, matrices, Plucker coordinates — all under one algebraic framework
questions.correctAnswer: Vectors, complex numbers, quaternions, matrices, Plucker coordinates — all under one algebraic framework
questions.explanation: GA: one algebra, one product (geometric product). Complex numbers = 2D even subalgebra. Quaternions = 3D even subalgebra. Unifies dot+cross.

questions.question: What is the geometric product of two vectors?
options: ab = a·b + a∧b — scalar (dot) PLUS bivector (wedge)
questions.correctAnswer: ab = a·b + a∧b — scalar (dot) PLUS bivector (wedge)
questions.explanation: Geometric product = symmetric part (dot, scalar) + antisymmetric part (wedge, bivector). Like complex numbers: real + imaginary.

questions.question: What are the grades in a 3D multivector?
options: Grade 0 (scalar), Grade 1 (vector, 3 components), Grade 2 (bivector, 3), Grade 3 (trivector, 1) — 2³=8 total
questions.correctAnswer: Grade 0 (scalar), Grade 1 (vector, 3 components), Grade 2 (bivector, 3), Grade 3 (trivector, 1) — 2³=8 total
questions.explanation: Pascal's triangle per dimension. 1,3,3,1 for 3D. Total = 2^dimension. Multivector = sum of all grades.

questions.question: Why is the cross product a 3D-only accident?
options: Cross product = Hodge dual of wedge: a×b = (a∧b)*. Only in 3D does the dual of a bivector give a vector. Wedge works in any dimension.
questions.correctAnswer: Cross product = Hodge dual of wedge: a×b = (a∧b)*. Only in 3D does the dual of a bivector give a vector. Wedge works in any dimension.
questions.explanation: In 3D: bivectors have 3 components = vectors have 3 components → duality possible. In 4D: bivectors have 6 components → can't map to 4-vector.

questions.q: How do complex numbers relate to GA?
o: Complex numbers ARE the even subalgebra of 2D GA — scalar + bivector. a+bi ↔ a + b·e₁₂.
questions.a: Complex numbers ARE the even subalgebra of 2D GA — scalar + bivector. a+bi ↔ a + b·e₁₂.
questions.e: 2D GA: basis {1, e₁, e₂, e₁₂}. Even subalgebra: {1, e₁₂}. e₁₂² = -1. This IS the complex numbers. i = e₁₂.

questions.question: What is the C++ Primer designed for?
options: Experienced C++ developers looking for a reference
Absolute beginners with zero programming experience
questions.correctAnswer: Absolute beginners with zero programming experience
questions.explanation: The note states: 'The entry point for absolute beginners. These notes assume zero prior experience.'

questions.question: What approach does the Primer take to teaching?
options: Hardware-first — starting from transistors and bytes
questions.correctAnswer: Hardware-first — starting from transistors and bytes
questions.explanation: The note: 'They start from hardware — what a transistor does, how a byte stores a number, what happens when you press compile.'

questions.question: How many notes are in the Primer reading sequence (excluding the overview)?
questions.explanation: The reading order table lists 6 notes: Variables, Control Flow, Functions, Arrays & Vectors, Pointers & References, Compiling & Running.

options: Follow the Learning Path to the full C++ Fundamentals
Start writing a game engine immediately

questions.question: What is the graphics pipeline from vertices to pixels?
options: Vertex shader → rasterizer → pixel shader → output merger. Programmable stages (shaders) + fixed-function (rasterizer).
questions.correctAnswer: Vertex shader → rasterizer → pixel shader → output merger. Programmable stages (shaders) + fixed-function (rasterizer).
questions.explanation: Vertex shader: transform vertices. Rasterizer: generate fragments. Pixel shader: compute color. Output merger: depth test, blend. UE's RHI abstracts this.

questions.q: What does fixed-function hardware do in the GPU pipeline?
o: Rasterizer (triangles→fragments), output merger (depth test, blend) — not programmable, just configurable
questions.a: Rasterizer (triangles→fragments), output merger (depth test, blend) — not programmable, just configurable
questions.e: Fixed-function = non-programmable stages. Rasterizer: generates fragments from triangles. Output merger: depth/stencil test, blending. Extremely fast dedicated silicon.

questions.q: What are the programmable stages of the GPU pipeline?
questions.correctAnswer: Vertex shader, hull shader, domain shader, geometry shader, pixel shader, compute shader
acceptableAnswers: vertex shader pixel shader compute shader
questions.explanation: Vertex: transform vertices. Pixel: color fragments. Compute: arbitrary parallel work. Hull/Domain: tessellation. Geometry: primitive manipulation.

questions.q: What happens in the rasterizer stage?
o: Converts triangles to pixel fragments — determines which pixels are covered, interpolates vertex attributes across triangle
questions.a: Converts triangles to pixel fragments — determines which pixels are covered, interpolates vertex attributes across triangle
questions.e: Rasterizer = fixed-function. Triangle setup → edge walking → fragment generation. Interpolates depth, UV, color, normal per fragment.

questions.q: What is a render target?
o: Memory buffer GPU renders into — can be framebuffer (screen), texture, or multiple targets (MRT).
questions.a: Memory buffer GPU renders into — can be framebuffer (screen), texture, or multiple targets (MRT).
questions.e: Render target = output surface. Color buffer, depth buffer, stencil. Deferred rendering uses multiple targets (GBuffer).

questions.question: What makes A* different from Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
questions.correctAnswer: A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
questions.explanation: f(n) = g(n) + h(n). g = cost from start. h = estimated cost to goal. If h is admissible (never overestimates), A* finds optimal path.

questions.question: What makes a heuristic admissible for A*?
options: h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
questions.correctAnswer: h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
questions.explanation: Admissible = optimistic. Euclidean distance ≤ actual path distance. Manhattan distance on grid = admissible (but not on diagonals).

questions.question: What data structure makes A* efficient?
options: Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
questions.correctAnswer: Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
questions.explanation: Open set = min-heap by f(n). Extract-min gives best candidate. Closed set = visited nodes. O(b^d) worst case; heuristic reduces this dramatically.

questions.q: What is a navigation mesh (navmesh)?
o: A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids
questions.a: A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids
questions.e: Navmesh: convex polygons = nodes. Shared edges = connections. Much fewer nodes than grid cells. UE generates navmesh from level geometry.

questions.q: What is the difference between A* on a grid vs a navmesh?
o: Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.
questions.a: Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.
questions.e: Navmesh drastically reduces search space. UE's Recast/Detour generates navmeshes. Pathfinding on navmesh = string-pulling for smooth final path.

questions.question: How do you test if two AABBs overlap?
options: Check if intervals overlap on ALL three axes: (a_min ≤ b_max) AND (a_max ≥ b_min) for x, y, z
questions.correctAnswer: Check if intervals overlap on ALL three axes: (a_min ≤ b_max) AND (a_max ≥ b_min) for x, y, z
questions.explanation: AABB-AABB = 6 comparisons (2 per axis). O(1). Separating axis: if any axis has gap, no overlap. All 3 overlap = collision.

questions.question: How do you find the closest point on an AABB to a point?
options: Clamp each coordinate: x = clamp(px, min.x, max.x), same for y, z
questions.correctAnswer: Clamp each coordinate: x = clamp(px, min.x, max.x), same for y, z
questions.explanation: Clamp to each axis-aligned interval. Result is the point ON or INSIDE the AABB closest to the query point. Basis of sphere-AABB test.

questions.q: How do you test sphere-sphere collision?
o: Distance between centers < sum of radii. O(1), one distance calculation.
questions.a: Distance between centers < sum of radii. O(1), one distance calculation.
questions.e: |c1 - c2| < r1 + r2. Square both sides to avoid sqrt: |c1-c2|² < (r1+r2)². Fastest collision test. Use for broad-phase rough checks.

questions.q: How do you test sphere-AABB collision?
questions.correctAnswer: Find closest point on AABB to sphere center (clamp each coordinate), then check distance to that point < sphere radius
acceptableAnswers: closest point on AABB then distance check
questions.explanation: 1. Clamp sphere center to AABB min/max → closest point. 2. Distance from closest point to center < radius = collision. O(1).

questions.q: What is the advantage of AABBs over OBBs?
o: AABB: axis-aligned — intersection test is 6 comparisons (O(1)). OBB: oriented — SAT needs 15 axes. AABB much faster, less precise.
questions.a: AABB: axis-aligned — intersection test is 6 comparisons (O(1)). OBB: oriented — SAT needs 15 axes. AABB much faster, less precise.
questions.e: AABB = cheap, fast, recompute on rotation. OBB = rotates with object, tighter fit, more expensive. Broad phase uses AABBs.

questions.q: How does sphere-sphere collision work?
o: Distance between centers < sum of radii. Square both sides to avoid sqrt. O(1), fastest collision test.
questions.a: Distance between centers < sum of radii. Square both sides to avoid sqrt. O(1), fastest collision test.
questions.e: |c1-c2|² < (r1+r2)². No SQRT needed. 3 subtractions + 3 multiplies + 2 additions + 1 compare. ~7 operations total.

questions.question: Why does UE disable C++ exceptions?
options: Performance predictability, code size, and console platform constraints
questions.correctAnswer: Performance predictability, code size, and console platform constraints
questions.explanation: Exceptions add 5-15% binary size and make control flow unpredictable. UE uses macros instead.

questions.question: What does check(expr) do?
options: If false → crashes immediately with message + callstack — in dev AND shipping builds
questions.correctAnswer: If false → crashes immediately with message + callstack — in dev AND shipping builds
questions.explanation: check() is fatal in all builds. ensure() logs and continues (dev only). verify() like check but expr runs in shipping too.

questions.question: How does UE's Cast<T>() work without RTTI?
options: Uses compile-time static class flags generated by UHT — O(1) type check
questions.correctAnswer: Uses compile-time static class flags generated by UHT — O(1) type check
questions.explanation: UHT generates StaticClass() and TCastFlags. Cast<T>() compares flags — no vtable walk, no RTTI overhead.

questions.question: What UE type replaces std::string?
questions.correctAnswer: FString
questions.explanation: FString = mutable heap string (like std::string). FName = interned string (O(1) comparison). FText = localizable.

questions.question: What is the difference between FName and FString?
options: FName is an interned, immutable string — O(1) comparison via index; FString is a regular mutable string
questions.correctAnswer: FName is an interned, immutable string — O(1) comparison via index; FString is a regular mutable string
questions.explanation: FName stores strings in a global table. Comparing two FNames is comparing integers (table indices).

questions.question: Why can't you use raw AActor* to keep a UObject alive in UE?
options: Only UPROPERTY() members and TObjectPtr prevent GC — raw pointers don't count as references
questions.correctAnswer: Only UPROPERTY() members and TObjectPtr prevent GC — raw pointers don't count as references
questions.explanation: Note: 'A raw AActor* does NOT prevent garbage collection. Only UPROPERTY() and TObjectPtr<T> keep UObjects alive.'

questions.question: What UE type replaces std::shared_ptr for non-UObject types?
questions.correctAnswer: TSharedPtr
questions.explanation: TSharedPtr is reference-counted like shared_ptr but for non-UObject types. TObjectPtr is for UObject GC tracking.

questions.question: What does ensure() do differently from check()?
options: Logs error + callstack but continues execution — only in development builds, stripped from shipping
questions.correctAnswer: Logs error + callstack but continues execution — only in development builds, stripped from shipping
questions.explanation: ensure() is for non-fatal errors you want to know about during development without crashing. Stripped from shipping.

questions.question: What physically stores a bit in DRAM?
options: A capacitor holding or not holding charge (1T1C cell)
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor holding or not holding charge (1T1C cell)
questions.explanation: DRAM = 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit. Capacitor charged = 1, discharged = 0. Must be refreshed every ~64ms.

questions.question: Why is DRAM called 'dynamic'?
options: Capacitor charge leaks out — must be refreshed every ~64ms or data is lost. Unlike SRAM which holds state.
questions.correctAnswer: Capacitor charge leaks out — must be refreshed every ~64ms or data is lost. Unlike SRAM which holds state.
questions.explanation: Dynamic = decays. Refresh reads and rewrites every row every 64ms. ~4.5% of DRAM time lost to refresh at normal temp.

questions.question: What is the row buffer and why does it matter?
options: When a row is opened, ALL columns are read into sense amplifiers. Subsequent accesses to same row are ~14ns vs ~40ns for closed row.
questions.correctAnswer: When a row is opened, ALL columns are read into sense amplifiers. Subsequent accesses to same row are ~14ns vs ~40ns for closed row.
questions.explanation: Row buffer hit = ~14ns (column access). Row buffer miss = tRCD + CL = ~40ns (precharge + row activate + column). 3x difference.

questions.question: Why is DRAM latency ~80-100ns while L1 cache is ~1ns?
options: Physical distance (15-25cm PCB trace = ~1ns minimum), DRAM array sensing (~50ns), bus transfer overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Physical distance (15-25cm PCB trace = ~1ns minimum), DRAM array sensing (~50ns), bus transfer overhead
questions.explanation: Four reasons: distance (speed of light), DRAM array internal slowness (tRCD + CL + tRP), pin bandwidth bottleneck, bank precharge.

questions.question: What is the memory wall?
questions.correctAnswer: CPU can execute ~400 instructions in the time of one DRAM access (100ns at 4GHz). Every cache level exists to avoid hitting DRAM.
acceptableAnswers: CPU executes 400 instructions per DRAM access
CPU is much faster than RAM
the gap between CPU speed and memory latency
questions.explanation: 4 instructions/ns × 100ns = 400 instructions per DRAM access. All caching, prefetching, data-oriented design exists because of this.

questions.question: What does the Fourier Transform convert?
options: Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
questions.correctAnswer: Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
questions.explanation: DFT: X[k] = Σx[n]·e^(-i·2π·k·n/N). Maps N time samples to N frequency bins. Magnitude = amplitude, angle = phase.

questions.question: Why is the FFT O(N log N) instead of O(N²)?
options: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
questions.correctAnswer: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
questions.explanation: X[k] = E[k] + W_N^k·O[k]; X[k+N/2] = E[k] - W_N^k·O[k]. Butterfly operations. log₂(N) stages. N=1024: DFT=1M ops, FFT=10K ops.

questions.question: How does the convolution theorem enable fast reverb?
options: Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
questions.correctAnswer: Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
questions.explanation: Dry audio ∗ impulse response → FFT(audio)·FFT(IR) → IFFT. 10,000× faster for real-time convolution reverb.

questions.question: What is a spectrogram?
options: 2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
questions.correctAnswer: 2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
questions.explanation: Spectrogram = STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform). Window function (Hann) reduces leakage. Hop size controls time resolution. Used in audio editors.

questions.q: What window function is commonly used before FFT and why?
o: Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries
questions.a: Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries
questions.e: Window functions prevent spectral leakage from the discontinuity at frame boundaries. Hann, Hamming, Blackman are common choices.

questions.question: What are the three mechanical operations that dominate HDD latency?
questions.correctAnswer: Seek (move head to track), rotational latency (wait for sector to spin under head), and transfer (read data)
questions.explanation: Seek ~8-9ms, rotational ~4ms (7200 RPM), transfer ~16µs for 4KB. 99.9% of latency is mechanical.

questions.question: Why is sequential access 100-750x faster than random access on HDD?
options: Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
questions.correctAnswer: Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
questions.explanation: Sequential: 250 MB/s. Random 4KB: ~0.33 MB/s (~83 IOPS × 4KB). Ratio ~750:1. Entirely due to mechanical seek+rotation.

questions.question: What does 'Advanced Format' / 4K native sectors mean?
options: Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
questions.correctAnswer: Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
questions.explanation: 512e drives: logical 512, physical 4096. A 512-byte write triggers RMW of full 4KB sector. Align partitions to 4KB.

questions.question: What is the areal density of modern HDDs?
options: How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
questions.correctAnswer: How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
questions.explanation: Higher areal density = more bits pass under the head per rotation. Primary driver of HDD throughput improvements.

questions.question: Why is append-only writing fast on HDD?
options: Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
questions.correctAnswer: Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
questions.explanation: Append = sequential write at current position. In-place = seek + rotational latency. Difference: 100-1000x.

questions.question: What three registers matter most for function calls?
questions.correctAnswer: RIP (instruction pointer — next instruction), RSP (stack pointer — top of stack), RBP (base pointer — frame anchor)
acceptableAnswers: instruction pointer, stack pointer, base pointer
questions.explanation: RIP: what to execute next. RSP: current stack top. RBP: fixed reference within frame. RSP moves; RBP stays fixed.

questions.question: What does the function prologue do?
options: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes new base, allocates locals
questions.correctAnswer: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes new base, allocates locals
questions.explanation: Prologue = enter function. push rbp saves old frame. mov rbp,rsp sets new frame. sub rsp,N allocates N bytes for locals.

questions.question: What does the function epilogue do?
options: mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret — deallocates locals, restores caller's frame, pops return address
questions.correctAnswer: mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret — deallocates locals, restores caller's frame, pops return address
questions.explanation: Epilogue = leave function. reverse of prologue. leave = mov rsp,rbp + pop rbp (one byte vs three).

questions.question: Why is stack allocation so fast?
options: Single sub instruction on RSP — no syscall, no malloc, no GC. Deallocation = add. One cycle.
questions.correctAnswer: Single sub instruction on RSP — no syscall, no malloc, no GC. Deallocation = add. One cycle.
questions.explanation: sub rsp, 4096 = allocate 4KB in one instruction. add rsp, 4096 = free. Compare: malloc(4096) = potentially syscall + page table updates.

questions.question: What is frame pointer omission (-fomit-frame-pointer)?
options: RBP freed as general-purpose register (~6% more GPRs). Locals accessed via RSP offsets. DWARF unwind tables needed for debugging.
questions.correctAnswer: RBP freed as general-purpose register (~6% more GPRs). Locals accessed via RSP offsets. DWARF unwind tables needed for debugging.
questions.explanation: -O2 default. RBP becomes general register. Debugger can't follow RBP chain — needs .eh_frame metadata. Release builds harder to debug.

questions.question: What does virtual enable?
options: Runtime dispatch — function called depends on actual object type, not pointer type
questions.correctAnswer: Runtime dispatch — function called depends on actual object type, not pointer type
questions.explanation: 'virtual: Don't decide based on pointer type. Look at actual object at runtime.'

questions.question: What does override do?
options: Compiler verifies function actually overrides base virtual — catches typos and signature mismatches
questions.correctAnswer: Compiler verifies function actually overrides base virtual — catches typos and signature mismatches
questions.explanation: 'Always use override. Catches typos, wrong params, wrong const, missing virtual in base.'

questions.question: Why virtual destructor required?
options: Delete through base pointer calls correct derived destructor chain
questions.correctAnswer: Delete through base pointer calls correct derived destructor chain
questions.explanation: Without virtual destructor, delete Base* only calls ~Base(), leaking derived resources.

questions.question: What is object slicing?
options: Copying a derived object to a base object — derived members are sliced off
questions.correctAnswer: Copying a derived object to a base object — derived members are sliced off
questions.explanation: 'Enemy e; Character c = e; — SLICED! c is just Character. Enemy members lost.'

questions.question: What does final do on a class?
options: Prevents further inheritance — no class can derive from it
questions.correctAnswer: Prevents further inheritance — no class can derive from it
questions.explanation: final on class = no more derived classes. final on virtual function = no further overrides.

questions.question: What defines a cubic Bezier curve?
options: 4 control points — endpoints (P0,P3) the curve passes through, handles (P1,P2) that pull the curve
questions.correctAnswer: 4 control points — endpoints (P0,P3) the curve passes through, handles (P1,P2) that pull the curve
questions.explanation: Cubic Bezier: B(t) = (1-t)³P0 + 3(1-t)²tP1 + 3(1-t)t²P2 + t³P3. Tangent at t=0: 3(P1-P0). Tangent at t=1: 3(P3-P2).

questions.question: What is the advantage of Catmull-Rom over Bezier?
options: Catmull-Rom passes THROUGH all control points (interpolating). Bezier points are 'magnets' — curve doesn't pass through handles.
questions.correctAnswer: Catmull-Rom passes THROUGH all control points (interpolating). Bezier points are 'magnets' — curve doesn't pass through handles.
questions.explanation: Catmull-Rom = interpolating spline. Each segment uses 4 points, passes through middle two. Tangent at P_i = (P_i+1 - P_i-1)/2.

questions.question: What is de Casteljau's algorithm?
options: Recursive linear interpolation to evaluate Bezier curves — numerically stable, avoids computing Bernstein polynomials
questions.correctAnswer: Recursive linear interpolation to evaluate Bezier curves — numerically stable, avoids computing Bernstein polynomials
questions.explanation: de Casteljau: repeatedly lerp between adjacent control points until one remains. O(n²) but numerically stable. Geometric intuition: fold paper.

questions.q: What is a B-spline?
o: Basis spline — generalization with local control (moving one point affects only nearby segments) and C² continuity
questions.a: Basis spline — generalization with local control (moving one point affects only nearby segments) and C² continuity
questions.e: B-splines: don't necessarily pass through control points. Local control = editing one CV changes only local region. NURBS adds weights.

questions.q: Why is uniform t-stepping not constant speed along a Bezier?
o: |B'(t)| varies with t — the derivative magnitude changes, so equal Δt covers different distances
questions.a: |B'(t)| varies with t — the derivative magnitude changes, so equal Δt covers different distances
questions.e: Speed = |dB/dt|. This is not constant. Arc-length reparameterization fixes this by driving t as a function of desired distance.

questions.question: What does std::move actually do?
options: Casts to rvalue reference (T&&), enabling move semantics
questions.correctAnswer: Casts to rvalue reference (T&&), enabling move semantics
questions.explanation: 'std::move is a cast. It doesn't move anything — it enables the move constructor to be selected.'

questions.question: What's the difference between std::move and std::forward?
options: move unconditionally casts to rvalue; forward conditionally casts preserving value category
questions.correctAnswer: move unconditionally casts to rvalue; forward conditionally casts preserving value category
questions.explanation: move = always rvalue. forward = lvalue stays lvalue, rvalue stays rvalue (perfect forwarding).

questions.question: What is an rvalue reference (T&&)?
options: Reference that binds to temporaries — enables moving from them
questions.correctAnswer: Reference that binds to temporaries — enables moving from them
questions.explanation: T&& binds to rvalues (temporaries, std::move results). Key to move semantics: steal resources, don't copy.

questions.question: What does a moved-from object contain?
options: Valid but unspecified state — typically empty/null/zero
questions.correctAnswer: Valid but unspecified state — typically empty/null/zero
questions.explanation: Moved-from objects are in a valid-but-unspecified state. Can be destroyed or assigned to. Don't rely on specific values.

questions.question: What is guaranteed about a moved-from std::unique_ptr?
options: It is nullptr — the moved-to pointer now owns the object
questions.correctAnswer: It is nullptr — the moved-to pointer now owns the object
questions.explanation: unique_ptr guarantees moved-from state is nullptr. Other types are in valid-but-unspecified state.

questions.question: What is a rotor in Geometric Algebra?
options: The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
questions.correctAnswer: The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
questions.explanation: Rotor: R = cos(θ/2) - sin(θ/2)B where B is unit bivector. Same 4 components as quaternion. Isomorphic to quaternions.

questions.question: What does the sandwich product RvR̃ do?
options: Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
questions.correctAnswer: Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
questions.explanation: Sandwich = universal GA pattern. Reflection: -nvn. Rotation: RvR̃ (two reflections). Translation: TvT̃. Everything is a sandwich.

questions.question: How are rotors more general than quaternions?
options: Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
questions.correctAnswer: Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
questions.explanation: Dimension-independence is the rotor's key advantage. 2D rotor = 2 components (complex). 3D rotor = 4 components (quaternion). 4D rotor = 8 components.

questions.q: What does a∧b = 0 mean geometrically?
o: a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area
questions.a: a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area
questions.e: Wedge product = 0 iff vectors are linearly dependent (parallel). a∧b = |a||b|sin(θ)·B. sin(0)=0, sin(180)=0.

questions.q: What is the pseudoscalar I in 3D GA?
o: I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).
questions.a: I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).
questions.e: I = Hodge dual operator. v·I converts vector to bivector. I²=-1 makes it behave like imaginary unit. Basis of complex/quaternion behavior in GA.

questions.question: What is Perlin noise?
options: Gradient-based smooth noise — places random gradient vectors at grid points, interpolates dot products. Natural-looking.
questions.correctAnswer: Gradient-based smooth noise — places random gradient vectors at grid points, interpolates dot products. Natural-looking.
questions.explanation: Ken Perlin 1983. Gradient at each lattice point. Dot with offset vector. Smoothstep interpolation. Zero at integer grid points.

questions.q: What is fractal noise (fBm)?
o: Sum of multiple noise octaves at different scales: Σ amplitude·noise(frequency·x). Amplitude halves each octave, frequency doubles.
questions.a: Sum of multiple noise octaves at different scales: Σ amplitude·noise(frequency·x). Amplitude halves each octave, frequency doubles.
questions.e: Fractal Brownian Motion. Octaves=detail layers. Lacunarity=frequency multiplier. Gain=amplitude衰减. Terrain = 4-8 octaves.

questions.q: What is Worley/Voronoi noise?
o: Distance to nearest random feature point — creates cell-like patterns (stone, skin, organic)
questions.a: Distance to nearest random feature point — creates cell-like patterns (stone, skin, organic)
questions.e: Voronoi: random points in cells. F1 = nearest distance. F2-F1 = edge intensity. Produces cellular/crack patterns.

questions.q: What is domain warping?
o: Apply noise to the INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.a: Apply noise to the INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.e: warp(x,y) = noise(x + noise(x+a,y+b), y + noise(x+c,y+d)). Distorted input → organic swirls. Used for smoke, clouds, lava.

questions.q: What is domain warping?
o: Apply noise to INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.a: Apply noise to INPUT coordinates of another noise — creates flowing, organic, marble-like patterns
questions.e: warp(x,y) = noise(x + noise1, y + noise2). Nested noise distortion. Produces swirling, fluid-like textures. Used for smoke, clouds.

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
options: Process has its own memory space; threads share memory within a process
questions.correctAnswer: Process has its own memory space; threads share memory within a process
questions.explanation: Process = independent memory space. Threads within a process share the same address space (heap, globals).

questions.question: What does each thread have that is private?
options: Its own stack and register state
questions.correctAnswer: Its own stack and register state
questions.explanation: Each thread has its own stack and CPU registers. Heap and globals are shared across all threads in the process.

questions.question: What does std::thread::join() do?
options: Blocks the calling thread until the spawned thread finishes
questions.correctAnswer: Blocks the calling thread until the spawned thread finishes
questions.explanation: join() waits for the thread to complete. Must be called before the std::thread object is destroyed.

questions.question: What happens if a std::thread object is destroyed without join() or detach()?
options: std::terminate() — the thread is still joinable
questions.correctAnswer: std::terminate() — the thread is still joinable
questions.explanation: A std::thread destructor calls terminate() if the thread is still joinable. Always join() or detach() before destruction.

questions.question: What is thread overhead?
options: Creating a thread allocates a stack (~1-8MB) and requires kernel scheduling — much heavier than a function call
questions.correctAnswer: Creating a thread allocates a stack (~1-8MB) and requires kernel scheduling — much heavier than a function call
questions.explanation: Thread creation is expensive. Thread pools reuse threads to amortize this cost.

questions.question: What does Newton's Second Law (F=ma) govern in physics engines?
options: Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
questions.correctAnswer: Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
questions.explanation: Forces cause acceleration. Gravity: F=mg. Friction, springs, constraints all produce forces. Integration accumulates force effects over time.

questions.question: What is the inertia tensor?
options: 3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
questions.correctAnswer: 3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
questions.explanation: Rotational analog of mass. Inertia tensor depends on shape and mass distribution. Diagonalized in principal axes. Harder to rotate around axes with larger inertia.

questions.q: What is angular velocity?
o: ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).
questions.a: ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).
questions.e: ω is a 3D vector. Direction = axis of rotation (right-hand rule). magnitude = radians per second. L = I·ω (angular momentum).

questions.q: What is the inertia tensor?
o: 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
questions.a: 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
questions.e: Rotational mass. Sphere: diagonal (all equal). Box: diagonal (different per axis). Non-diagonal for arbitrary shapes. Diagonalized in body frame.

questions.q: What are the six degrees of freedom of a rigid body?
questions.correctAnswer: 3 translational (x, y, z) + 3 rotational (pitch, yaw, roll)
acceptableAnswers: x y z pitch yaw roll
questions.explanation: 6 DOF = complete description of position and orientation in 3D space. A rigid body's state = position(3) + velocity(3) + orientation(4) + angularVelocity(3).

questions.q: What is the center of mass?
o: The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).
questions.a: The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).
questions.e: COM = weighted average of mass distribution. Linear dynamics use COM position and velocity. Rotation is around COM.

questions.question: What is Linear Blend Skinning (LBS)?
options: Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
questions.correctAnswer: Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
questions.explanation: Each vertex influenced by 1-4 bones. Bone weights sum to 1. GPU blends 4 matrices per vertex. Fast but causes candy-wrapper effect.

questions.question: What is the candy-wrapper effect in LBS?
options: When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
questions.correctAnswer: When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
questions.explanation: Cause: SO(3) not closed under addition. Blending two rotation matrices does NOT produce a rotation matrix. Determinant < 1 = volume collapse.

questions.question: How do dual quaternions fix the candy-wrapper effect?
options: Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
questions.correctAnswer: Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
questions.explanation: DQS: convert bone transforms to dual quaternions, blend, normalize. Result always rigid. ~2× cost of LBS. UE supports both.

questions.question: What is the bind pose?
options: The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
questions.correctAnswer: The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
questions.explanation: Bind pose = rest pose. Inverse bind matrices (IB_i) map vertices to bone-local space IN the bind pose. Computed once at import.

questions.question: How many bones typically influence a single vertex?
options: 4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
questions.explanation: UE default: 4 influences per vertex. 8 possible with higher quality settings. Artists paint weights in Maya/Blender. Red=strong, blue=weak.

questions.question: What is the fundamental speed difference between stack and heap allocation?
options: Stack: one CPU instruction (move RSP); heap: searching free space, ~50-200+ cycles
questions.correctAnswer: Stack: one CPU instruction (move RSP); heap: searching free space, ~50-200+ cycles
questions.explanation: Note: 'Stack allocation is automatic, one CPU instruction. Heap involves searching for free space.'

questions.question: What determines a stack variable's lifetime?
options: Tied to function scope — destroyed when function returns
questions.correctAnswer: Tied to function scope — destroyed when function returns
questions.explanation: Note: 'Stack lifetime: tied to function scope. Heap: as long as you want — until delete.'

questions.question: What is the typical maximum stack size?
questions.correctAnswer: 1-8 MB (platform-dependent)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Stack max ~1-8 MB. Heap: gigabytes. Large data must go on heap.'

questions.question: What register tracks the top of the stack on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: RSP
questions.explanation: Note: 'The CPU tracks the top with a register called the stack pointer (RSP on x86-64).'

questions.question: Why is the stack cache-friendly?
options: LIFO — the top is always in L1/L2 cache, recently used memory is hot
questions.correctAnswer: LIFO — the top is always in L1/L2 cache, recently used memory is hot
questions.explanation: The stack's LIFO nature means the most recently pushed data stays cache-hot.

questions.question: What fragmentation pattern does the stack have?
questions.correctAnswer: None — LIFO allocation/deallocation prevents fragmentation
questions.explanation: Note table: Stack fragmentation = None (LIFO). Heap can fragment over time.

questions.question: What must be known at compile time for a stack variable?
questions.correctAnswer: Its size (for arrays/VLAs)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Variable size must be known at compile time on the stack. Heap can be determined at runtime.'

questions.question: At the hardware level, what physically stores a single bit in DRAM?
options: A capacitor holding or not holding charge
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor holding or not holding charge
questions.explanation: Note: 'RAM is a grid of billions of microscopic capacitors. One capacitor holds charge (1) or doesn't (0) = one bit.'

questions.question: How many bytes does `int score = 0;` reserve on modern platforms?
questions.correctAnswer: 4 bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'the compiler reserves 4 bytes (32 capacitors) in RAM' for an int.

questions.question: What three properties does every variable have?
questions.correctAnswer: A name, a type, and an address
acceptableAnswers: a name a type and an address
questions.explanation: Note: 'Every variable has three properties: a name, a type (how to interpret the bits), and an address (where in the capacitor grid).'

questions.question: How does the CPU allocate space for local variables on the stack?
options: Writes to a special allocation table
questions.correctAnswer: Subtracts from the RSP register
questions.explanation: Note: 'Allocation = subtract from RSP. Deallocation = add to RSP. One CPU instruction.'

questions.question: The same 32-bit pattern 0x42C80000 can represent different values depending on type. What value as a float?
questions.explanation: The type lens table shows: interpreted as float → 100.0. Same bits, different meaning.

questions.question: Why does `bool` waste 7 bits of a byte?
options: The CPU's smallest addressable unit is one byte — it cannot address individual bits
Compilers are lazy about bool optimization
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU's smallest addressable unit is one byte — it cannot address individual bits
questions.explanation: Note warns: 'CPU can't address bits. 8 bools = 8 bytes, not 1.'

questions.question: How are signed integers encoded in modern C++?
questions.correctAnswer: Two's complement
questions.explanation: Note: 'Negate: flip all bits, add 1.' Enables a single adder circuit for both signed and unsigned.

questions.question: What are the three fields of an IEEE 754 single-precision float?
questions.correctAnswer: Sign, exponent, and mantissa
questions.explanation: 32 bits: sign (1 bit), exponent (8 bits, bias 127), mantissa (23 bits with implicit leading 1).

questions.question: What does `sizeof` return and when is it evaluated?
options: The runtime size of a variable, evaluated at runtime
The compile-time size in bytes, evaluated by the compiler before code generation
The number of elements in an array
The alignment requirement of a type
questions.correctAnswer: The compile-time size in bytes, evaluated by the compiler before code generation
questions.explanation: Note: 'NOT a function call — evaluated at compile time. Zero runtime cost.'

questions.question: Why doesn't `0.1f + 0.2f == 0.3f`?
options: 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be exactly represented in binary floating point
The == operator doesn't work with floats
questions.correctAnswer: 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be exactly represented in binary floating point
questions.explanation: Note: 'Floats are approximate. Never use ==.' Binary FP can't exactly represent 0.1 or 0.2.

questions.question: Why must RSP be 16-byte aligned at call?
options: SSE movaps requires 16-byte aligned memory — faults on misaligned addresses. ABI mandates alignment.
questions.correctAnswer: SSE movaps requires 16-byte aligned memory — faults on misaligned addresses. ABI mandates alignment.
questions.explanation: call pushes 8 bytes (return address), misaligning to 8 mod 16. push rbp adds 8, realigning to 16. Locals rounded to 16-byte multiples.

questions.question: What is the red zone (System V only)?
options: 128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
questions.correctAnswer: 128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
questions.explanation: Red zone = System V only. Windows has NO red zone — below RSP is volatile (interrupts may overwrite). Cross-platform code: never assume it.

questions.question: What is the shadow space (Microsoft x64)?
options: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
questions.correctAnswer: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
questions.explanation: Shadow space = 32 bytes reserved by caller. Simplifies variadic functions and debugging. System V has no shadow space.

questions.question: How does System V differ from MS x64 for argument registers?
options: SysV: RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 (6 regs). MS: RCX,RDX,R8,R9 (4 regs). SysV has red zone; MS has shadow space.
questions.correctAnswer: SysV: RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 (6 regs). MS: RCX,RDX,R8,R9 (4 regs). SysV has red zone; MS has shadow space.
questions.explanation: Key differences: 6 vs 4 arg registers, RDI/RSI callee-saved on Windows (caller-saved on SysV — #1 porting trap), red zone vs shadow space.

questions.question: Why does Windows x64 use only 4 argument registers vs System V's 6?
options: Historical continuity with x86 __fastcall (ECX,EDX). Shadow space handles spill. Different design tradeoffs.
questions.correctAnswer: Historical continuity with x86 __fastcall (ECX,EDX). Shadow space handles spill. Different design tradeoffs.
questions.explanation: MS extended x86 __fastcall. AMD defined SysV ABI for 64-bit; Linux/macOS adopted it. Microsoft created their own. Historical accident.

questions.question: What are the stages of compilation in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler (front-end, optimizer, back-end), Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: .cpp → preprocessor (.i) → compiler: front-end (AST), optimizer (IR), back-end (assembly) → assembler (.o) → linker (executable).

questions.question: What does the compiler front-end do?
options: Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
questions.correctAnswer: Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
questions.explanation: Front-end: lexer → tokens, parser → AST, semantic analysis → typed AST, IR generation. This is where C++ syntax errors are caught.

questions.question: What does the optimizer do?
options: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
questions.correctAnswer: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
questions.explanation: Optimizer works on IR (GIMPLE/LLVM IR). Most passes are target-independent. -O0 skips most; -O2 is standard; -O3 is aggressive.

questions.question: What does the linker do?
options: Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
questions.correctAnswer: Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
questions.explanation: Each .o has symbol table: exports (defines) and imports (references). Linker matches them. Static: copies code. Dynamic: PLT/GOT stubs.

questions.question: What is an object file (.o)?
options: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
questions.correctAnswer: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
questions.explanation: .o contains compiled code but unresolved symbols. Linker combines .o files, resolves relocations, produces executable.

questions.question: What does RIP hold?
options: The address of the next instruction to execute
questions.correctAnswer: The address of the next instruction to execute
questions.explanation: Note: 'RIP holds the address of the next instruction. Control flow is anything that makes RIP jump.'

questions.question: What assembly instructions does an `if` statement compile to?
options: cmp followed by a conditional jump (jle, jg, etc.)
questions.correctAnswer: cmp followed by a conditional jump (jle, jg, etc.)
questions.explanation: Note: 'The hardware doesn't know if. It knows cmp (subtract, set flags) and jmp variants.'

questions.question: Which CPU flag is set when a comparison produces a result of zero?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF (Zero Flag)
questions.explanation: Note: 'ZF (Zero Flag): result = 0 (values equal).' The je instruction tests this flag.

questions.question: Why is a `switch` with dense case values faster than a long `if-else` chain?
options: The compiler unrolls the switch into parallel comparisons
Switch uses a jump table — O(1) indexed jump instead of O(N) sequential comparisons
Switch statements are compiled to GPU shaders
The CPU has a special SWITCH instruction
questions.correctAnswer: Switch uses a jump table — O(1) indexed jump instead of O(N) sequential comparisons
questions.explanation: Note: 'switch on dense values = jump table. One subtraction, one range check, one indexed jump. O(1).'

questions.question: What is the typical cycle penalty for a branch misprediction?
questions.correctAnswer: ~15-20 cycles
questions.explanation: Note: 'if wrong flushes the pipeline — ~15-20 cycle penalty.'

questions.question: Which loop construct saves one `jmp` instruction compared to `while`?
questions.correctAnswer: do-while
questions.explanation: Note: 'do-while — Saves One Jump. No initial forward conditional jump because the body always executes at least once.'

questions.question: Do `for` and `while` loops produce different assembly code for equivalent logic?
options: It depends on the compiler optimization level
questions.correctAnswer: No — they produce identical assembly
questions.explanation: Note: 'for produces EXACTLY the same machine code as the equivalent while. Syntactic sugar — no performance difference.'

questions.question: What do `break` and `continue` compile to?
options: They are handled entirely by the optimizer with no assembly representation
questions.correctAnswer: Unconditional jmp instructions — single cycle
questions.explanation: Note: 'Both compile to single unconditional jmp instructions — not function calls, zero overhead.'

questions.question: Why does sorting data before a branch-heavy loop sometimes make it faster?
options: Sorted data compresses better in cache
The branch predictor thrives on patterns — sorted data creates predictable branches
The compiler optimizes sorted loops differently
It eliminates the need for branching entirely
questions.correctAnswer: The branch predictor thrives on patterns — sorted data creates predictable branches
questions.explanation: Note: 'Sorting data before processing can make a loop faster because the branch predictor thrives on patterns.'

questions.question: What happens when a `switch` case has no `break`?
options: Execution falls through to the next case
questions.correctAnswer: Execution falls through to the next case
questions.explanation: Note: 'Without break, execution falls through — deliberate C heritage for shared handlers.'

questions.question: What are the four compilation stages in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler, Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: .cpp → Preprocessor (.i) → Compiler (.s) → Assembler (.o) → Linker (.exe).

questions.question: What does the preprocessor do with #include?
options: Copy-pastes the entire file at the directive position
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-pastes the entire file at the directive position
questions.explanation: #include = text paste. <iostream> adds ~25,000 lines before the compiler sees it.

questions.question: What does the linker resolve?
options: Symbols — connects 'require' in one .o to 'provide' in another .o or library
questions.correctAnswer: Symbols — connects 'require' in one .o to 'provide' in another .o or library
questions.explanation: Each .o has a symbol table: what it provides (defines) and what it requires (declared but not defined). Linker matches them.

questions.question: What does the OS loader do before main() runs?
options: Creates process with private virtual address space, maps .text/.data/.bss/stack/heap, loads dynamic libs
questions.correctAnswer: Creates process with private virtual address space, maps .text/.data/.bss/stack/heap, loads dynamic libs
questions.explanation: Loader: create process → map segments → load dynamic libraries → jump to _start → __libc_start_main → main().

questions.question: What file extension does the assembler output?
questions.correctAnswer: .o / .obj
questions.explanation: Assembler: .s (assembly text) → .o (binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries).

questions.question: What does the compiler's optimizer do?
options: Inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, vectorization — transforms IR to faster equivalent
questions.correctAnswer: Inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, vectorization — transforms IR to faster equivalent
questions.explanation: Compiler: C++ → AST → IR (GIMPLE/LLVM IR) → optimized IR → target assembly. Optimization happens at IR level.

questions.question: What is an 'undefined reference' linker error?
options: A symbol was declared but never defined — the linker can't find its .o or library
questions.correctAnswer: A symbol was declared but never defined — the linker can't find its .o or library
questions.explanation: Undefined reference = linker found a 'require' with no matching 'provide'. Forgot to link a .cpp or library.

questions.question: What does the capture [] control?
options: Which external variables lambda can access and how (by value or reference)
questions.correctAnswer: Which external variables lambda can access and how (by value or reference)
questions.explanation: Capture list: [=] copies everything, [&] references everything, [x, &y] mixed.

questions.question: What is the danger of [&] in async lambdas?
options: Dangling references — if lambda outlives captured variables
questions.correctAnswer: Dangling references — if lambda outlives captured variables
questions.explanation: 'Capturing by Reference Is Dangerous. If referenced variable destroyed before lambda runs → UB.'

questions.question: What does mutable do on a lambda?
options: Allows modifying captured-by-value variables inside the lambda
questions.correctAnswer: Allows modifying captured-by-value variables inside the lambda
questions.explanation: By default, lambda operator() is const. mutable removes that const.

questions.question: What does std::function cost compared to a template parameter?
options: Virtual-like dispatch + potential heap allocation for large captures
questions.correctAnswer: Virtual-like dispatch + potential heap allocation for large captures
questions.explanation: std::function uses type erasure. Prefer template parameters for performance-critical code.

questions.question: What is a generic lambda (C++14)?
options: Lambda with auto parameters — generates templated operator()
questions.correctAnswer: Lambda with auto parameters — generates templated operator()
questions.explanation: [](auto a, auto b) { return a + b; } generates a template operator(). Works with any types.

questions.question: What is the fundamental asymmetry of NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: Read/program per page (~4-16KB), erase per block (hundreds of pages). Cannot overwrite — must erase first.
acceptableAnswers: read program per page erase per block
read and write per page, erase per block
questions.explanation: Page = read/write unit (~25-100µs read, ~200-1500µs write). Block = erase unit (512-2048 pages, ~1.5-10ms).

questions.question: What is write amplification?
options: A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
questions.correctAnswer: A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
questions.explanation: Overwrite → old page stale, write to new page. GC: copy valid pages, erase block. User 4KB may cause 2MB+ of NAND writes.

questions.question: What does the Flash Translation Layer (FTL) do?
options: Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
questions.correctAnswer: Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
questions.explanation: FTL = SSD's firmware brain. LBA→physical mapping, wear leveling, GC, bad block management. OS sees a block device.

questions.question: Why does SLC endure 50-100x more writes than QLC?
options: SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
questions.correctAnswer: SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
questions.explanation: SLC: 50K-100K P/E cycles. QLC: ~500-1000. More bits per cell = tighter voltage discrimination = faster wear-out.

questions.question: Why do SSDs slow down when nearly full?
options: Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
questions.correctAnswer: Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
questions.explanation: 90% full SSD: few pre-erased blocks. GC must reclaim before writes. Over-provisioning (7-28% hidden spare) mitigates.

questions.question: What is Euler integration?
options: Simplest: pos += vel·dt; vel += acc·dt. O(dt) error per step. Unstable for stiff systems.
questions.correctAnswer: Simplest: pos += vel·dt; vel += acc·dt. O(dt) error per step. Unstable for stiff systems.
questions.explanation: Explicit Euler: first-order. Cheap but diverges for oscillatory systems unless dt is very small. Game physics at 60Hz often uses semi-implicit Euler.

questions.question: What is semi-implicit Euler (symplectic)?
options: vel += acc·dt FIRST, then pos += vel·dt. More stable than explicit Euler for same cost. Standard in game physics.
questions.correctAnswer: vel += acc·dt FIRST, then pos += vel·dt. More stable than explicit Euler for same cost. Standard in game physics.
questions.explanation: Symplectic Euler preserves energy better than explicit. Order matters: update velocity first, then use NEW velocity for position. UE physics uses this.

questions.q: What is Verlet integration?
o: pos(t+dt)=2·pos(t)-pos(t-dt)+acc·dt². Velocity-free, time-reversible, good energy conservation.
questions.a: pos(t+dt)=2·pos(t)-pos(t-dt)+acc·dt². Velocity-free, time-reversible, good energy conservation.
questions.e: Verlet: no explicit velocity. Velocity = (pos(t+dt)-pos(t-dt))/(2·dt). Used in molecular dynamics, cloth simulation.

questions.q: What is fixed vs variable timestep in physics?
o: Fixed: deterministic, reproducible. Variable: frame-rate matched. UE: fixed physics timestep with sub-stepping for stability.
questions.a: Fixed: deterministic, reproducible. Variable: frame-rate matched. UE: fixed physics timestep with sub-stepping for stability.
questions.e: Fixed timestep = stability, reproducibility. Variable = frame-synced. UE physics runs at fixed rate (default 60Hz) with accumulator for sub-steps.

questions.q: Why does explicit Euler gain energy?
o: Each step slightly overshoots the true trajectory — error accumulates, orbits expand, springs explode. Semi-implicit fixes this.
questions.a: Each step slightly overshoots the true trajectory — error accumulates, orbits expand, springs explode. Semi-implicit fixes this.
questions.e: Explicit Euler evaluates derivative at START of step. Semi-implicit uses updated velocity for position — much better energy behavior.

questions.q: What is RK4 integration?
o: 4th-order Runge-Kutta: evaluates derivative at 4 points per step, weighted average. O(dt⁵) error. Expensive but very accurate.
questions.a: 4th-order Runge-Kutta: evaluates derivative at 4 points per step, weighted average. O(dt⁵) error. Expensive but very accurate.
questions.e: RK4: 4 evaluations per step. k1=f(t,y), k2=f(t+dt/2,y+k1·dt/2), k3, k4. Average with weights [1,2,2,1]. Used where accuracy > speed.

questions.q: What is the spring-mass-damper system?
o: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement, damping proportional to velocity. Stiff springs need small dt.
questions.a: F = -k·x - c·v. Spring force proportional to displacement, damping proportional to velocity. Stiff springs need small dt.
questions.e: Hooke's law + damping. k=stiffness, c=damping. Stiff springs (large k) require tiny dt for explicit methods. Implicit methods handle stiff springs better.

questions.question: Why is sizeof(struct{char a; int b; char c;}) typically 12, not 6?
options: Padding — CPU requires aligned access; compiler inserts invisible bytes
questions.correctAnswer: Padding — CPU requires aligned access; compiler inserts invisible bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'Six bytes of data, twelve bytes of object. Padding. A 4-byte int must live at an address divisible by 4.'

questions.question: What hardware constraint drives alignment requirements?
options: CPUs read memory in aligned word chunks — misaligned data requires two reads
questions.correctAnswer: CPUs read memory in aligned word chunks — misaligned data requires two reads
questions.explanation: Note: 'CPUs don't read byte-by-byte. Misaligned int crossing a word boundary needs two memory accesses.'

questions.question: How can you reduce padding in a struct?
options: Order members from largest to smallest alignment
questions.correctAnswer: Order members from largest to smallest alignment
questions.explanation: Placing larger-alignment members first reduces padding gaps.

questions.question: What is the alignment of a double on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 8 bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'A type of size N must live at an address divisible by N (up to pointer size).' double is 8 bytes.

questions.question: What does AoS vs SoA stand for and which is faster for SIMD?
options: Array of Structures vs Structure of Arrays — SoA is often faster for SIMD
questions.correctAnswer: Array of Structures vs Structure of Arrays — SoA is often faster for SIMD
questions.explanation: Structure of Arrays keeps same-type fields contiguous for better cache and SIMD utilization.

questions.question: What does `alignof(T)` return?
options: The required memory alignment of type T — the address must be divisible by this value
The number of T objects in a cache line
The offset of T within a struct
questions.correctAnswer: The required memory alignment of type T — the address must be divisible by this value
questions.explanation: Note: alignof returns the alignment requirement. alignof(int) = 4, alignof(double) = 8.

questions.question: What happens to a struct's size when you use `#pragma pack(1)`?
options: All padding is removed — members are packed tightly with no gaps
questions.correctAnswer: All padding is removed — members are packed tightly with no gaps
questions.explanation: #pragma pack(1) forces byte-alignment, removing padding. Tradeoff: potentially slower access due to misalignment.

questions.question: What is Projective Geometric Algebra (PGA)?
options: Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
questions.correctAnswer: Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
questions.explanation: PGA: adds projective dimension. Points are intersections of 3 planes. Lines = join of 2 points. Ray-plane intersection = meet(L,Π). One operation.

questions.question: What is a motor in PGA?
options: Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
questions.correctAnswer: Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
questions.explanation: Motor slerp: one interpolation for rotation AND translation. No separate lerp(pos) + slerp(quat). Unified rigid body interpolation.

questions.question: How does PGA express ray-plane intersection?
options: L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
questions.correctAnswer: L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
questions.explanation: Join (∧) creates higher grade. Meet (∨) = intersection. Ray = join(camera, farPoint). Hit = meet(ray, plane). All algebra, no special cases.

questions.q: Why is e₀² = 0 in PGA?
o: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.
questions.a: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.
questions.e: PGA = Cl(3,0,1) — degenerate metric. e₀²=0 enables translation via rotor-like sandwich. Same algebra models points, lines, planes, and rigid motions.

questions.q: How many components does a PGA motor have?
o: 8 (scalar + 6 bivectors + pseudoscalar) — isomorphic to dual quaternion
questions.a: 8 (scalar + 6 bivectors + pseudoscalar) — isomorphic to dual quaternion
questions.e: Motor = even-grade element in PGA. 8 components: 1 scalar, 3 e₀ᵢ bivectors (translation), 3 eᵢⱼ bivectors (rotation), 1 e₀₁₂₃. Represents rigid body motion.

questions.question: What is virtual memory's fundamental trick?
options: Every process thinks it owns ALL memory starting at address 0. The OS silently translates virtual→physical addresses.
questions.correctAnswer: Every process thinks it owns ALL memory starting at address 0. The OS silently translates virtual→physical addresses.
questions.explanation: Virtual address space = private illusion. MMU translates every access. Each process has its own page table → different physical frames.

questions.question: How many levels does x86-64 page table have?
options: 4 (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT) — 9 bits each, 12-bit offset = 48-bit virtual address
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT) — 9 bits each, 12-bit offset = 48-bit virtual address
questions.explanation: 4-level radix tree. Each level = 512 entries. 4 sequential memory accesses for one translation. TLB caches recent translations.

questions.question: What is the TLB and why is it critical?
options: Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small hardware cache (64 entries L1) for recent virtual→physical translations. ~1 cycle hit.
questions.correctAnswer: Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small hardware cache (64 entries L1) for recent virtual→physical translations. ~1 cycle hit.
questions.explanation: TLB hit = ~1 cycle. TLB miss = 4-level page walk (~50-400ns). Without TLB, the MMU would need 4 memory accesses per translation.

questions.question: How much memory does a 64-entry L1 D-TLB cover with 4KB pages?
options: 256 KB (64 × 4KB) — very small! This is why huge pages matter.
questions.correctAnswer: 256 KB (64 × 4KB) — very small! This is why huge pages matter.
questions.explanation: 64 entries × 4KB = 256KB. If your working set is larger, TLB misses dominate. Solution: 2MB huge pages → 128MB coverage.

questions.question: What are the three key reasons virtual memory exists?
questions.correctAnswer: Isolation (processes can't access each other), simplification (programs start at address 0), overcommit (OS can promise more than physical RAM)
questions.explanation: Isolation = process can't read/write other processes. Simplification = every program thinks it starts at 0. Overcommit = allocate 10GB, use 2GB.

questions.question: What is the slab method for ray-AABB intersection?
options: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. Overlap of all intervals = hit. t_entry ≤ t_exit for all 3 axes.
questions.correctAnswer: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. Overlap of all intervals = hit. t_entry ≤ t_exit for all 3 axes.
