questions.question: What kinds of resources can RAII manage?
questions.correctAnswer: Memory, files, locks, sockets, database connections, GPU buffers — anything with acquire/release
questions.explanation: Note lists: 'Memory (new/delete), files (open/close), locks (lock/unlock), sockets, database connections, GPU buffers.'

questions.question: What happens to RAII-managed resources if an exception is thrown?
options: Destructors still run during stack unwinding — resources are released
questions.correctAnswer: Destructors still run during stack unwinding — resources are released
questions.explanation: Note: 'Even if an exception is thrown, destructors run during stack unwinding. Resources are always released.'

questions.question: What types can structured bindings work with?
options: Pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members
Any type with a default constructor
questions.correctAnswer: Pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members
questions.explanation: Note: 'Structured bindings work with: pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members.'

questions.question: What three specializations enable custom types for structured bindings?
questions.correctAnswer: std::tuple_size, std::tuple_element, and get<I>()
questions.explanation: Note: specialize std::tuple_size (count), std::tuple_element<I,T> (type per index), provide get<I>(T) via ADL.

questions.question: What does the compiler generate for `auto [x, y] = GetPair();`?
options: A hidden copy with x and y as references into it
x and y moved from the pair
questions.correctAnswer: A hidden copy with x and y as references into it
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto __hidden = GetPair(); then references: x = get<0>(__hidden), y = get<1>(__hidden).'

questions.question: How does `const auto& [x, y]` differ from `auto [x, y]`?
options: x and y are const references — no copy is made
questions.correctAnswer: x and y are const references — no copy is made
questions.explanation: Note: 'const auto& [cx, cy, cz] = p; // const references — no copy.' Auto applies to hidden object.

questions.question: What C++20 feature uses structured bindings in a switch?
options: Switch with init-statement: switch (auto [code, msg] = GetResult(); code)
questions.correctAnswer: Switch with init-statement: switch (auto [code, msg] = GetResult(); code)
questions.explanation: Note: 'C++20: switch (auto [code, msg] = GetResult(); code) { case 0: ... case 404: ... }'

questions.question: In what order do structured bindings map to struct members?
questions.correctAnswer: Declaration order
questions.explanation: Note: 'Bindings map to member names in declaration order — NOT alphabetical.' First declared member = first binding.

questions.question: What is the aggregate protocol for structured bindings?
options: A special interface you must implement
If a type has only public members and no user-declared constructors, bindings work automatically
questions.correctAnswer: If a type has only public members and no user-declared constructors, bindings work automatically
questions.explanation: Note: 'Aggregate protocol: if all members are public and no user constructors, structured bindings work automatically.'

questions.question: What is a syscall?
options: A controlled transition from user mode (ring 3) to kernel mode (ring 0) — the only way user code accesses hardware/OS services
questions.correctAnswer: A controlled transition from user mode (ring 3) to kernel mode (ring 0) — the only way user code accesses hardware/OS services
questions.explanation: Syscall = user→kernel transition. Examples: read(), write(), open(), mmap(). syscall instruction on x86-64. Cost: ~100-300 cycles.

questions.question: What are CPU protection rings?
options: Privilege levels: ring 0 (kernel, full access), ring 3 (user, restricted). Syscalls transition 3→0.
questions.correctAnswer: Privilege levels: ring 0 (kernel, full access), ring 3 (user, restricted). Syscalls transition 3→0.
questions.explanation: x86 has 4 rings but only 0 and 3 are used. Ring 0: execute privileged instructions, access all memory. Ring 3: restricted.

questions.question: How does the SYSCALL instruction work?
options: Atomically: saves RIP to RCX, loads RIP from LSTAR MSR (kernel handler), switches to ring 0. SYSRET reverses.
questions.correctAnswer: Atomically: saves RIP to RCX, loads RIP from LSTAR MSR (kernel handler), switches to ring 0. SYSRET reverses.
questions.explanation: SYSCALL: RCX←RIP, RIP←LSTAR, R11←RFLAGS, RFLAGS←RFLAGS & ~FMASK. No stack switch (uses MSRs). ~100-300 cycles.

questions.question: Why are syscalls expensive?
options: Mode switch (ring 3→0), register save/restore, kernel validation of arguments, potentially TLB flush, Spectre mitigations
questions.correctAnswer: Mode switch (ring 3→0), register save/restore, kernel validation of arguments, potentially TLB flush, Spectre mitigations
questions.explanation: ~100-300 cycles minimum. More with KPTI (Meltdown mitigation: page tables switch). VDSO maps some syscalls to userspace for speed.

questions.question: What is io_uring (Linux)?
options: A high-performance async I/O interface using shared ring buffers — submit and complete queues mapped in userspace, minimal syscalls
questions.correctAnswer: A high-performance async I/O interface using shared ring buffers — submit and complete queues mapped in userspace, minimal syscalls
questions.explanation: io_uring: submission queue (SQ) and completion queue (CQ) shared between kernel and userspace. Batch I/O ops with minimal syscall overhead.

questions.question: Key difference between variant and C union?
options: variant has discriminator tracking active alternative — type-safe; C unions don't
questions.correctAnswer: variant has discriminator tracking active alternative — type-safe; C unions don't
questions.explanation: variant stores max(sizeof(alternatives)) + discriminator. Always knows which type is active.

questions.question: Does std::optional allocate on heap?
options: No — inline storage, sizeof ≈ sizeof(T) + alignment padding
questions.correctAnswer: No — inline storage, sizeof ≈ sizeof(T) + alignment padding
questions.explanation: 'optional is NOT a pointer. No heap allocation. Stores value inline.' sizeof(optional<int>) ≈ 8.

questions.question: What is std::visit used for?
options: Compile-time exhaustive dispatch over all variant alternatives
questions.correctAnswer: Compile-time exhaustive dispatch over all variant alternatives
questions.explanation: visit generates dispatch table. If you add a new alternative, visit won't compile until handled. Exhaustive.

questions.question: When should you use std::any?
options: When types aren't enumerable at compile time — scripting, plugin APIs
questions.correctAnswer: When types aren't enumerable at compile time — scripting, plugin APIs
questions.explanation: any uses heap + RTTI. Prefer variant for closed type sets, optional for nullable values.

questions.question: What does std::optional::value_or(default) do?
options: Returns contained value if present, otherwise returns the default
questions.correctAnswer: Returns contained value if present, otherwise returns the default
questions.explanation: value_or() is the safe accessor: get the value or a fallback without checking or throwing.

questions.question: What is CMake?
options: A meta-build system — generates native build files (Makefiles, VS solutions) from CMakeLists.txt
questions.correctAnswer: A meta-build system — generates native build files (Makefiles, VS solutions) from CMakeLists.txt
questions.explanation: CMake reads CMakeLists.txt and outputs platform-specific build files. It doesn't build directly.

questions.question: Why should you build out-of-source?
options: Keeps generated files separate from source — can delete build/ and regenerate cleanly
questions.correctAnswer: Keeps generated files separate from source — can delete build/ and regenerate cleanly
questions.explanation: mkdir build && cd build && cmake .. — all generated files in build/. Never build in the source directory.

questions.question: What does target_link_libraries do?
options: Specifies that a target depends on another library — linker will resolve symbols from it
questions.correctAnswer: Specifies that a target depends on another library — linker will resolve symbols from it
questions.explanation: target_link_libraries(MyGame PRIVATE Engine) tells CMake MyGame needs Engine. PRIVATE = only MyGame needs it.

questions.question: What is the difference between STATIC and SHARED libraries?
options: STATIC = copied into executable at link time; SHARED = loaded at runtime (.dll/.so)
questions.correctAnswer: STATIC = copied into executable at link time; SHARED = loaded at runtime (.dll/.so)
questions.explanation: add_library(Engine STATIC ...) vs SHARED. Static increases binary size; shared reduces it but adds runtime dependency.

questions.question: What does CMake's configure step do?
options: Generates build files — reads CMakeLists.txt, detects compiler, finds dependencies, creates Makefiles/sln
questions.correctAnswer: Generates build files — reads CMakeLists.txt, detects compiler, finds dependencies, creates Makefiles/sln
questions.explanation: cmake .. is configure. cmake --build . is build. They're separate steps — reconfigure only when CMakeLists.txt changes.

questions.question: What three keywords identify C++20 coroutines?
questions.correctAnswer: co_await, co_yield, co_return
questions.explanation: co_await suspends/resumes, co_yield produces value and suspends, co_return completes the coroutine.

questions.question: What is a coroutine?
options: A function that can suspend and resume — like a function with multiple entry/exit points
questions.correctAnswer: A function that can suspend and resume — like a function with multiple entry/exit points
questions.explanation: Coroutines can pause mid-execution and resume later. Enables cooperative multitasking without threads.

questions.question: What does co_yield do?
options: Produces a value and suspends — for generators
questions.correctAnswer: Produces a value and suspends — for generators
questions.explanation: co_yield returns a value to the caller and suspends. The coroutine can be resumed to yield more values.

questions.question: What does co_await do?
options: Suspends execution until the awaited operation completes
questions.correctAnswer: Suspends execution until the awaited operation completes
questions.explanation: co_await is the primary coroutine keyword: suspend until the awaitable produces a result.

questions.question: Can C++ coroutines run on multiple threads?
options: They can — the coroutine itself is stackless; resumption can happen on any thread
questions.correctAnswer: They can — the coroutine itself is stackless; resumption can happen on any thread
questions.explanation: C++20 coroutines are stackless. The coroutine frame is heap-allocated. Resumption can happen from any thread.

questions.question: What is livelock?
options: A deadlock variant where threads are blocked
Threads are actively running but constantly yielding to each other — making zero net progress
questions.correctAnswer: Threads are actively running but constantly yielding to each other — making zero net progress
questions.explanation: Livelock: threads respond to each other's actions but no forward progress. Different from deadlock (threads not blocked).

questions.question: What is starvation?
options: A thread is ready but never gets the mutex because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
questions.correctAnswer: A thread is ready but never gets the mutex because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
questions.explanation: Starvation: low-priority thread never gets CPU or lock. Fix: fair scheduling or priority inheritance.

questions.question: What are the four Coffman conditions for deadlock?
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
acceptableAnswers: mutual exclusion hold-and-wait no preemption circular wait
mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
questions.explanation: All four must hold for deadlock. Break any one to prevent deadlock. Lock ordering breaks circular wait.

questions.question: How does consistent lock ordering prevent deadlock?
options: If all threads lock A before B, circular wait is impossible
questions.correctAnswer: If all threads lock A before B, circular wait is impossible
questions.explanation: Lock ordering eliminates the circular wait condition. Every thread acquires locks in the same order.

questions.question: What is the difference between deadlock and livelock at the CPU level?
options: Deadlock: threads in SLEEPING state (blocked). Livelock: threads in RUNNING state but making no progress.
questions.correctAnswer: Deadlock: threads in SLEEPING state (blocked). Livelock: threads in RUNNING state but making no progress.
questions.explanation: Deadlock = blocked in FUTEX_WAIT. Livelock = executing CAS loops, burning CPU, zero progress.

questions.question: What does `int* p = &x;` do?
options: Creates a copy of x named p
Stores the memory address of x in p
Creates an alias p that IS x
Initializes p to the same value as x
questions.correctAnswer: Stores the memory address of x in p
questions.explanation: Note: 'p stores the ADDRESS of x. & = address of.' The diagram shows p contains 0x7FFF1000.

questions.question: How many memory accesses does `*p = 100` require at the assembly level?
options: Two — one to load the address from p, one to write 100 to that address
Zero — the compiler optimizes it away
questions.correctAnswer: Two — one to load the address from p, one to write 100 to that address
questions.explanation: Note assembly: 'mov rax, [p]' (load address from p) then 'mov [rax], 100' (write) — two memory accesses.

questions.question: What does pointer arithmetic `p + n` actually compute?
questions.correctAnswer: p + n × sizeof(*p) bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'p + n advances by n × sizeof(element) bytes.' For int* p, p+2 advances 8 bytes (2 × 4).

questions.question: Which of these are equivalent ways to access the 3rd element of an array?
questions.correctAnswer: arr[2], *(p + 2), *(arr + 2), and 2[arr]
questions.explanation: Note: 'arr[2] ≡ *(p + 2) ≡ *(arr + 2) ≡ 2[arr] — all identical.' arr[i] = *(arr + i).

questions.question: What happens when you dereference a nullptr?
options: Undefined behavior — typically a crash (segfault/access violation)
The compiler prevents this at compile time
questions.correctAnswer: Undefined behavior — typically a crash (segfault/access violation)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Dereferencing nullptr → undefined behavior (typically crash).'

questions.question: Why do smart pointers prevent memory leaks that raw pointers can't?
options: Their destructor automatically deletes the managed object when the pointer goes out of scope
They store data on the stack instead of the heap
They use reference counting for everything
questions.correctAnswer: Their destructor automatically deletes the managed object when the pointer goes out of scope
questions.explanation: Note: 'Smart pointer destructor automatically deletes the managed object. Raw pointers never auto-delete.'

questions.question: What does `void*` represent?
options: A generic pointer that can hold any address — must be cast before dereferencing
A pointer to a void function
questions.correctAnswer: A generic pointer that can hold any address — must be cast before dereferencing
questions.explanation: Note: void* is a type-erased pointer. You can't dereference it without casting to a concrete type first.

questions.question: What is the difference between `int* p` and `int *p`?
options: int* p is preferred in C++
There is no difference — the compiler treats them identically
int* p declares a pointer; int *p dereferences
questions.correctAnswer: There is no difference — the compiler treats them identically
questions.explanation: Note: Both are equivalent. The * binds to the variable name, not the type — 'int *p, x' declares one pointer and one int.

questions.question: When are templates instantiated?
options: At compile time, at point of use — compiler generates copy per type
questions.correctAnswer: At compile time, at point of use — compiler generates copy per type
questions.explanation: Templates instantiated at point of use. Compiler needs full definition visible (why templates go in headers).

questions.question: What problem do C++20 concepts solve?
options: Incomprehensible error messages — concepts produce clean errors at call site
questions.correctAnswer: Incomprehensible error messages — concepts produce clean errors at call site
questions.explanation: 'The biggest template problem: 300 lines of error trace. Concepts: clean message at the call site.'

questions.question: What is a variadic template?
options: Template accepting any number of type/argument parameters using ...
questions.correctAnswer: Template accepting any number of type/argument parameters using ...
questions.explanation: 'Args... is a parameter pack — captures any number of types. Fold expressions (C++17) operate on packs.'

questions.question: What does if constexpr do?
options: Compile-time branch — discarded branch doesn't even need to compile for given type
questions.correctAnswer: Compile-time branch — discarded branch doesn't even need to compile for given type
questions.explanation: if constexpr evaluates condition at compile time. Discarded branch is completely eliminated.

questions.question: Why must template code go in headers?
options: Compiler needs full definition at instantiation point — can't link against pre-compiled template
questions.correctAnswer: Compiler needs full definition at instantiation point — can't link against pre-compiled template
questions.explanation: Templates are instantiated at use. The definition must be visible. Put in headers or .inl files.

questions.question: What does `auto` strip from the deduced type?
questions.correctAnswer: References and top-level const
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto strips references and const (top-level). const int& → auto deduces int.'

questions.question: How do you make `auto` preserve references?
questions.correctAnswer: auto&
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto& r = GetValue(); // r is const int& — reference preserved.'

questions.question: What type does `decltype(x)` deduce for `int x = 5;`?
questions.correctAnswer: int
questions.explanation: Note: 'decltype deduces x's declared type — int.' Unlike auto, decltype preserves references and const.

questions.question: What is `decltype(auto)` used for?
options: Perfect return type forwarding — preserves references and const
questions.correctAnswer: Perfect return type forwarding — preserves references and const
questions.explanation: Note: 'decltype(auto) preserves everything. Use for perfect return type forwarding.'

questions.question: Why should you NOT use `for (auto x : vec)` to iterate?
options: It copies every element — use for (auto& x : vec)
questions.correctAnswer: It copies every element — use for (auto& x : vec)
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto never deduces to a reference. for (auto x : vec) copies every element.'

questions.question: What is the difference between `auto` and `decltype` regarding references?
options: auto strips references; decltype preserves them exactly as declared
decltype strips references; auto preserves them
auto is for variables; decltype is for functions
questions.correctAnswer: auto strips references; decltype preserves them exactly as declared
questions.explanation: Note: 'Unlike auto, decltype preserves references and const exactly as declared.'

questions.question: When is it NOT recommended to use auto?
options: When the return type isn't obvious from the initializer — the reader can't deduce what type it is
questions.correctAnswer: When the return type isn't obvious from the initializer — the reader can't deduce what type it is
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto result = initialize(); — reader has to deduce what initialize() returns. Make the type explicit.'

questions.question: What is an octree?
options: 3D spatial partitioning: recursively subdivide space into 8 octants. Each node has 0 or 8 children.
questions.correctAnswer: 3D spatial partitioning: recursively subdivide space into 8 octants. Each node has 0 or 8 children.
questions.explanation: Octree = 3D quadtree. Used for spatial indexing (collision, rendering), level-of-detail, voxel compression. UE uses for lightmass and some collision.

questions.q: What are voxels?
o: 3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.
questions.a: 3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.
questions.e: VOlumetric piXEL. Minecraft-style worlds, medical CT scans, UE Voxel Plugin. Marching Cubes converts voxels to meshes.

questions.q: What is an octree used for?
o: Spatial indexing: 3D recursive subdivision into 8 octants. Used for collision, LOD, voxel compression, light mapping
questions.a: Spatial indexing: 3D recursive subdivision into 8 octants. Used for collision, LOD, voxel compression, light mapping
questions.e: Each node: 0 or 8 children. Subdivide when cell has too many objects. UE lightmass, some collision queries, voxel plugins all use octrees.

questions.q: How does sparse voxel octree (SVO) save memory?
o: Only store nodes that contain data — skip empty regions. Dense grid = O(n³); SVO = O(surface area).
questions.a: Only store nodes that contain data — skip empty regions. Dense grid = O(n³); SVO = O(surface area).
questions.e: SVO: only allocate where geometry exists. Vast empty spaces = no nodes. Enables high-resolution voxel data without memory explosion.

questions.q: What is ray-voxel traversal?
o: Marching ray through voxel grid: step to next voxel boundary along each axis. Efficient for ray casting in voxel worlds.
questions.a: Marching ray through voxel grid: step to next voxel boundary along each axis. Efficient for ray casting in voxel worlds.
questions.e: DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer): advance ray to next voxel boundary. O(number of voxels traversed). Used in voxel ray tracing, Minecraft-style games.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of std::array over a C-style array?
options: Same memory layout but adds .size(), .at() bounds checking, never decays to pointer
questions.correctAnswer: Same memory layout but adds .size(), .at() bounds checking, never decays to pointer
questions.explanation: Zero-overhead: same contiguous memory as C array, but with container interface.

questions.question: What happens to a C array's size when passed to a function?
options: Decays to pointer — sizeof becomes 8 (pointer size), array size lost
questions.correctAnswer: Decays to pointer — sizeof becomes 8 (pointer size), array size lost
questions.explanation: C arrays decay to pointers. sizeof(arr) in a function returns pointer size, not array size.

questions.question: How do you get the size of a std::array at compile time?
questions.correctAnswer: All three work
questions.explanation: std::array::size() is constexpr. sizeof gives bytes. tuple_size gives element count at compile time.

questions.question: Can std::array be used with range-for loops?
options: Yes — it has begin() and end() iterators
questions.correctAnswer: Yes — it has begin() and end() iterators
questions.explanation: std::array is a full container with begin()/end(), works with range-for and all STL algorithms.

questions.question: Does `std::array<int, 5> arr;` initialize its elements?
options: No — elements are uninitialized (garbage) unless value-initialized with {}
questions.correctAnswer: No — elements are uninitialized (garbage) unless value-initialized with {}
questions.explanation: Like C arrays, std::array without initializer leaves elements uninitialized. Use {} for zero-initialization.

questions.question: What does std::atomic guarantee?
options: Atomicity — indivisible read-modify-write, no torn reads/writes, well-defined memory ordering
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity — indivisible read-modify-write, no torn reads/writes, well-defined memory ordering
questions.explanation: atomic is lock-free for fundamental types. Guarantees: no torn reads, indivisible RMW, controlled memory ordering.

questions.question: What does memory_order_relaxed provide?
options: Atomicity only — no ordering guarantees between threads. Counters where order doesn't matter.
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity only — no ordering guarantees between threads. Counters where order doesn't matter.
questions.explanation: relaxed = cheapest. Use for simple counters where only the final value matters, not the ordering relative to other operations.

questions.question: What does memory_order_seq_cst guarantee?
options: Single total order — all threads agree on the order of all seq_cst operations. Strongest, most expensive.
questions.correctAnswer: Single total order — all threads agree on the order of all seq_cst operations. Strongest, most expensive.
questions.explanation: seq_cst = default. MFENCE on x86 (~20-30 cycles). All threads see all seq_cst ops in the same order.

questions.question: How does acquire-release ordering work?
options: Release store synchronizes-with acquire load on same atomic — creates happens-before edge, transitively ordering all prior writes
questions.correctAnswer: Release store synchronizes-with acquire load on same atomic — creates happens-before edge, transitively ordering all prior writes
questions.explanation: acquire/release pair creates a synchronizes-with relationship. Classic pattern: flag.store(true, release); while(!flag.load(acquire)).

questions.question: Why is x86 'easy mode' for memory ordering?
options: x86 has Total Store Order (TSO) — stores appear in consistent order to all cores. acquire/release are free.
questions.correctAnswer: x86 has Total Store Order (TSO) — stores appear in consistent order to all cores. acquire/release are free.
questions.explanation: x86 TSO makes acquire/release semantically free. ARM/RISC-V are weakly ordered — require explicit barrier instructions.

questions.question: What are the four stages of the C++ compilation pipeline, in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler, Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: Note pipeline: .cpp → Preprocessor (.i) → Compiler (.s) → Assembler (.o) → Linker (.exe).

questions.question: What does the preprocessor do with `#include <iostream>`?
options: Imports only the declarations actually used
Copy-pastes the entire iostream file (~25,000 lines) into your source
Links to the iostream shared library at runtime
Generates a forward declaration for every symbol
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-pastes the entire iostream file (~25,000 lines) into your source
questions.explanation: Note: '#include — copy-paste entire file. Preprocessed: ~25,000 lines (entire iostream pasted).'

questions.question: At which stage do syntax errors and type errors get caught?
questions.correctAnswer: Compiler
questions.explanation: Note: 'Compiler errors occur here — syntax, type errors.' The compiler is the stage that understands C++.

questions.question: What does the linker do?
options: Resolves symbols — connects function calls to their definitions across object files
questions.correctAnswer: Resolves symbols — connects function calls to their definitions across object files
questions.explanation: Note: 'The linker resolves symbols. Connects declarations in one file to definitions in another.'

questions.question: What does an 'undefined reference' linker error mean?
options: You declared a function but never defined it anywhere the linker can find
The compiler ran out of memory
questions.correctAnswer: You declared a function but never defined it anywhere the linker can find
questions.explanation: Note: 'Undefined reference: you declared a function but didn't define it, or forgot to link the .cpp file.'

questions.question: What file extension does the assembler produce?
questions.correctAnswer: .o / .obj
questions.explanation: Note: Assembler converts .s (assembly) to .o (object file with machine code + symbol table).

questions.question: What is the difference between a .o file and an executable?
options: A .o file contains machine code with unresolved symbols; the executable has all symbols resolved and a main() entry point
A .o file is text; an executable is binary
A .o file is for debugging only
questions.correctAnswer: A .o file contains machine code with unresolved symbols; the executable has all symbols resolved and a main() entry point
questions.explanation: Note: Object files have machine code + symbol table with unresolved references. The linker resolves all symbols into the final executable.

questions.question: Which g++ flag shows the preprocessor output?
questions.correctAnswer: -E
questions.explanation: Note: 'View preprocessor output: g++ -E hello.cpp -o hello.i'. -S generates assembly, -c compiles to object, -o sets output filename.

questions.question: What two mechanisms enable type conversion in C++?
questions.correctAnswer: Conversion operators (in the source type) and converting constructors (in the destination type)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Either gate: conversion operator inside source type, OR converting constructor inside destination type.'

questions.question: What does `explicit` do to a constructor or conversion operator?
options: Disables implicit use — still available via direct-init Dest d(src) or static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.correctAnswer: Disables implicit use — still available via direct-init Dest d(src) or static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.explanation: Note: 'explicit disables silent conversion. Direct-init and static_cast still work.'

questions.question: Why does `explicit operator bool` work in `if (ptr)`?
options: Contextual conversion — the standard grants a special exception for boolean contexts
questions.correctAnswer: Contextual conversion — the standard grants a special exception for boolean contexts
questions.explanation: Note: 'Contextual conversion: explicit operator bool CAN be used implicitly — but ONLY in boolean contexts (if, while, &&, ||, !, ?:).'

questions.question: How many user-defined conversions will the compiler chain implicitly?
questions.correctAnswer: One
questions.explanation: Note: 'At most ONE user-defined conversion in an implicit sequence. A→B→C requires two — rejected.'

questions.question: What does C++20 `explicit(bool)` allow?
options: Conditional explicitness based on a compile-time predicate like is_convertible_v
questions.correctAnswer: Conditional explicitness based on a compile-time predicate like is_convertible_v
questions.explanation: Note: 'explicit(!is_convertible_v<T,int>) — safe for float, guarded for string.'

questions.question: What happens if both a conversion operator and a converting constructor are non-explicit?
options: Ambiguity error — the compiler refuses to choose
questions.correctAnswer: Ambiguity error — the compiler refuses to choose
questions.explanation: Note: 'Both non-explicit → ambiguity error. Fix: make one explicit, or refactor to have only one path.'

questions.question: What contexts does `explicit` NOT block?
options: Direct-initialization Dest d(src) and explicit casts static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.correctAnswer: Direct-initialization Dest d(src) and explicit casts static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.explanation: Note table shows: direct-initialization ✅ Allowed, explicit cast ✅ Allowed. Argument/return/copy-init are blocked.

questions.question: Why does UE prefer explicit on single-argument constructors?
options: Prevents accidental temporaries, makes allocations visible, defends against refactoring bugs
questions.correctAnswer: Prevents accidental temporaries, makes allocations visible, defends against refactoring bugs
questions.explanation: Note gives three reasons: prevents accidental temporaries, makes allocations visible, defends against refactoring bugs.

questions.question: Key advantage of ranges over iterator pairs?
options: Composable pipelines with lazy evaluation — filter|transform|take, zero intermediate allocations
questions.correctAnswer: Composable pipelines with lazy evaluation — filter|transform|take, zero intermediate allocations
questions.explanation: 'Ranges compose with |. filter | transform | take — lazy, no allocations. Zero-cost abstraction.'

questions.question: What is a view in ranges?
options: A lazy, non-owning range adaptor — computes on-demand, no storage
questions.correctAnswer: A lazy, non-owning range adaptor — computes on-demand, no storage
questions.explanation: Views are lightweight objects storing reference to underlying range + parameters. Pull model.

questions.question: What does rv::filter(pred) return?
options: A view — lazy, no allocation, filters on-demand

questions.question: What is a projection in std::ranges algorithms?

questions.question: What three pointers does std::vector store internally?

questions.question: What is the growth factor of std::vector?

questions.explanation: Must be exponential for amortized O(1) push_back. MSVC uses 1.5x for better memory reuse.

questions.question: What's the difference between size() and capacity()?
options: size() = stored elements; capacity() = allocated slots before reallocation
questions.correctAnswer: size() = stored elements; capacity() = allocated slots before reallocation

questions.correctAnswer: Pre-allocates capacity to at least n — prevents reallocations until size exceeds n
questions.explanation: reserve() is the key to vector performance: allocate once, push many times without reallocation.

questions.question: What does emplace_back do differently from push_back?

questions.question: What does deducing this eliminate?

questions.question: What monadic operation chains std::optional calls without nested ifs?

questions.question: Why is C++23 called a 'completion' release?

questions.question: What is a read-write lock?

questions.correctAnswer: Allows multiple simultaneous readers OR one exclusive writer — shared_mutex in C++17
questions.explanation: shared_mutex: lock_shared() for readers (concurrent), lock() for writers (exclusive). Good for read-heavy workloads.

questions.question: What is a semaphore (C++20)?

questions.explanation: counting_semaphore<N> limits concurrent access to N threads. Binary semaphore (N=1) is similar to mutex but not owned.

questions.question: What is a barrier (std::barrier, C++20)?

questions.correctAnswer: A synchronization point where ALL participating threads must arrive before ANY can proceed
questions.explanation: barrier blocks until N threads have arrived. Used for phased algorithms where all threads must complete phase N before starting N+1.

questions.question: What does std::call_once ensure?

questions.correctAnswer: A function is called exactly once, even with concurrent callers — thread-safe lazy initialization

questions.question: What makes an algorithm lock-free?

questions.correctAnswer: At least one thread makes progress in a bounded number of steps — no thread can block all others indefinitely

questions.question: What is CAS (Compare-And-Swap)?

questions.question: What is the advantage of lock-free over mutex-based code?

questions.correctAnswer: No thread can be blocked/suspended — immune to priority inversion, deadlock, and scheduler preemption mid-operation

questions.question: What is a hazard pointer?

questions.correctAnswer: A mechanism for safe memory reclamation in lock-free data structures — prevents use-after-free without GC
questions.explanation: Hazard pointers mark objects as 'in use' by a thread. Objects aren't freed until no thread has them hazard-marked.

questions.question: What is false sharing?

questions.question: When is a spinlock better than a mutex?

questions.question: What is work stealing?

questions.explanation: monotonic_buffer_resource = bump allocator. No individual frees. Reset by destroying the resource.

questions.question: What is the performance advantage of pmr + monotonic over default allocator?
options: Allocations are bump-pointer (extremely fast); no fragmentation; no per-object heap overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Allocations are bump-pointer (extremely fast); no fragmentation; no per-object heap overhead
questions.explanation: Monotonic: one subtraction per alloc. No heap walking. Benchmark: 3-10x faster than default allocator.

questions.question: What does unsynchronized_pool_resource do?
options: Pool of same-sized blocks — not thread-safe, designed for single-thread use
questions.correctAnswer: Pool of same-sized blocks — not thread-safe, designed for single-thread use
questions.explanation: unsynchronized = no internal locking. Faster for single-thread use. Use synchronized_pool_resource for thread safety.

questions.question: How do you use std::pmr with existing code that takes const vector<int>&?
options: pmr::vector<int> IS the same type — works with any memory_resource*
questions.correctAnswer: pmr::vector<int> IS the same type — works with any memory_resource*
questions.explanation: The killer feature: same type regardless of allocator. Functions taking pmr::vector<T> work with any backing resource.

questions.question: What is FName?
options: Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
questions.correctAnswer: Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
questions.explanation: FName: hash table index. Comparing two FNames = comparing integers. Used for asset paths, identifiers. Case-insensitive.

questions.q: What is FText?
o: Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.
questions.a: Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.
questions.e: FText = user-facing, localizable. FString = mutable, gameplay use. FName = interned, identifiers. Three distinct use cases.

questions.q: What is TCHAR?
o: UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.
questions.a: UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.
questions.e: TCHAR abstracts platform encoding. TEXT("hello") = L"hello" on Windows, "hello" elsewhere. FString is TArray<TCHAR>.

questions.q: Why is FName comparison O(1)?
o: FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)
questions.a: FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)
questions.e: FName = (index, number). Index into global TNameEntryArray. == operator compares indices. String comparison is never done at runtime.

questions.q: What is the FName pool and why does it matter?
o: A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).
questions.a: A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).
questions.e: FNames live forever in the global pool. Don't create FNames from dynamic strings (player names, chat messages). Use FString for runtime-generated text.

questions.question: Why are 4x4 matrices used for 3D transforms?
options: Homogeneous coordinates enable translation as a linear operation — without the 4th row/column, translation requires separate vector addition
questions.correctAnswer: Homogeneous coordinates enable translation as a linear operation — without the 4th row/column, translation requires separate vector addition
questions.explanation: 4x4 matrix: (x,y,z,1) × M. The 4th column encodes translation. 3x3 can't represent translation in a single matrix multiply.

questions.question: What is the order of the MVP transformation?
options: Model → World, World → View, View → Projection (M first, then V, then P)
questions.correctAnswer: Model → World, World → View, View → Projection (M first, then V, then P)
questions.explanation: MVP = Projection × View × Model. Applied right-to-left to vertex: P·V·M·v. Model: local→world. View: world→camera. Projection: camera→clip.

questions.question: Why is matrix multiplication not commutative?
options: Order matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate. AB ≠ BA for matrices.
questions.correctAnswer: Order matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate. AB ≠ BA for matrices.
questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication is composition of transformations. Rotating then translating moves in the rotated direction. Translating then rotating moves first, then rotates around origin.

questions.question: What does the inverse of a rotation matrix equal?
options: Its transpose — rotation matrices are orthogonal: R⁻¹ = R^T
questions.correctAnswer: Its transpose — rotation matrices are orthogonal: R⁻¹ = R^T
questions.explanation: Rotation matrices are orthonormal: columns are unit vectors, mutually perpendicular. R^T × R = I. Computing transpose is cheaper than full inverse.

questions.q: What is a basis vector?
o: The axes defining a coordinate system — (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1) in standard basis. Changing basis = changing coordinate system.
questions.a: The axes defining a coordinate system — (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1) in standard basis. Changing basis = changing coordinate system.
questions.e: A matrix's columns ARE its basis vectors. Applying a matrix to a vector expresses it in that matrix's coordinate system.

questions.question: Why does UE use MoveTemp instead of std::move?
options: UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
questions.correctAnswer: UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
questions.explanation: UE naming convention: MoveTemp for rvalue cast. MoveTempIfPossible for conditional move. All wrap standard move semantics.

questions.q: What does MoveTemp do?
o: UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.
questions.a: UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.
questions.e: MoveTemp = UE naming for std::move. MoveTempIfPossible = conditional move. Same underlying mechanics as standard move semantics.

questions.q: What does MoveTempIfPossible do?
o: Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.
questions.a: Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.
questions.e: MoveTempIfPossible checks TIsMoveConstructible<T>. Falls back to copy for non-movable types. Used in TArray growth path.

questions.q: Why is TArray always able to move on reallocation?
o: UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.
questions.a: UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.
questions.e: No exceptions = no exception-safety concern. TArray doesn't need move_if_noexcept. Always moves elements on reallocation.

questions.q: What is FORCEINLINE and why is it used on MoveTemp?
o: Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.
questions.a: Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.
questions.e: MoveTemp is a template that MUST be inlined. FORCEINLINE = __forceinline on MSVC, always_inline on Clang. Zero function call overhead.

questions.question: What are the three network roles in UE?
questions.correctAnswer: ROLE_Authority (server), ROLE_AutonomousProxy (owning client), ROLE_SimulatedProxy (other clients)
questions.explanation: Authority: server, has final say. Autonomous: client that owns the pawn. Simulated: everyone else's pawn. Role determines execution path.

questions.question: What does HasAuthority() check?
options: Is this machine the network authority (server) for this actor?
questions.correctAnswer: Is this machine the network authority (server) for this actor?
questions.explanation: HasAuthority() = GetLocalRole() == ROLE_Authority. Use this to guard server-only logic: spawning, damage, state changes.

questions.q: What does ROLE_AutonomousProxy mean?
o: The client that OWNS this actor — receives client RPCs, runs input prediction. Only one client has this role per actor.
questions.a: The client that OWNS this actor — receives client RPCs, runs input prediction. Only one client has this role per actor.
questions.e: AutonomousProxy = 'this is my pawn.' Input prediction runs here. Client RPCs delivered here. Server sees this actor as ROLE_Authority.

questions.q: What does GetLocalRole() return on a dedicated server?
o: ROLE_Authority — server is always the authority for all actors
questions.a: ROLE_Authority — server is always the authority for all actors
questions.e: Dedicated server: all actors have local role = ROLE_Authority. No autonomous proxies (no local player). No simulated proxies (no rendering).

questions.q: What ROLE does a pawn have on other clients?
o: ROLE_SimulatedProxy — other clients simulate the pawn based on replicated state, no input
questions.a: ROLE_SimulatedProxy — other clients simulate the pawn based on replicated state, no input
questions.e: SimulatedProxy = 'someone else's pawn.' Receives replicated position, velocity. No input processing. Smoothly interpolates server state.

questions.question: What is TObjectPtr?
options: GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
questions.correctAnswer: GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
questions.explanation: TObjectPtr<T> replaces raw T* in UE5. Implicit conversion to T*. GC tracking. Lazy object resolution. Editor cook integration.

questions.q: What is TSharedPtr?
o: Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr
questions.a: Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr
questions.e: TSharedPtr: non-UObject ref-counting. TSharedRef: non-nullable variant. TWeakPtr: non-owning observer. For UObjects use TObjectPtr.

questions.q: What is TUniquePtr?
o: Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.
questions.a: Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.
questions.e: TUniquePtr<T> = exclusive heap ownership. Cannot be copied. Move transfers ownership. Automatically deletes on destruction.

questions.q: What is TWeakObjectPtr?
o: Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.
questions.a: Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.
questions.e: TWeakObjectPtr = observer. Doesn't keep UObject alive. Used for optional references, back-pointers that shouldn't prevent GC.

questions.q: Why can't you use std::shared_ptr for UObjects?
o: UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.
questions.a: UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.
questions.e: UObject lifetime is managed by UE's garbage collector. Adding shared_ptr refcounting would create two competing ownership systems.

questions.question: What problem do quaternions solve that Euler angles don't?
options: Gimbal lock — when two rotation axes align, you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions avoid this entirely.
questions.correctAnswer: Gimbal lock — when two rotation axes align, you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions avoid this entirely.
questions.explanation: Gimbal lock: pitch=90° → yaw and roll become the same rotation. Quaternions use 4D hypersphere — no singularities.

questions.question: How many components does a quaternion have?
options: 4 (w, x, y, z) — scalar part w + vector part (x,y,z). Unit quaternion constraint: w²+x²+y²+z²=1
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (w, x, y, z) — scalar part w + vector part (x,y,z). Unit quaternion constraint: w²+x²+y²+z²=1
questions.explanation: Quaternion = cos(θ/2) + sin(θ/2)(x·i + y·j + z·k). 4 numbers, 1 constraint = 3 DOF for rotation. Same as rotation matrix but compact.

questions.question: What is SLERP?
options: Spherical Linear intERPolation — interpolates along the great circle on the 4D hypersphere, constant angular velocity
questions.correctAnswer: Spherical Linear intERPolation — interpolates along the great circle on the 4D hypersphere, constant angular velocity
questions.explanation: slerp(q0,q1,t) = (sin((1-t)Ω)/sin(Ω))·q0 + (sin(tΩ)/sin(Ω))·q1. Stays on unit hypersphere. Constant speed rotation.

questions.question: Why not just lerp quaternions?
options: lerp goes through interior of hypersphere — produces non-unit quaternions. Normalizing after (nlerp) fixes magnitude but not speed.
questions.correctAnswer: lerp goes through interior of hypersphere — produces non-unit quaternions. Normalizing after (nlerp) fixes magnitude but not speed.
questions.explanation: Lerp cuts chord through sphere. Nlerp normalizes but speeds up in middle. Slerp stays on surface at constant angular velocity.

questions.question: How are quaternions related to rotation matrices?
options: A unit quaternion q can be converted to a 3x3 rotation matrix. The conversion formula involves q's components in a specific pattern.
questions.correctAnswer: A unit quaternion q can be converted to a 3x3 rotation matrix. The conversion formula involves q's components in a specific pattern.
questions.explanation: Quaternion→matrix: standard formula. Quaternion rotation: v' = q·v·q⁻¹ (sandwich product). Two quat multiplies vs 9 multiplies + 6 adds for matrix×vector.

questions.question: What does UFUNCTION(Server) do?
options: Marks a function as Server RPC — called on client, executes on server. UFUNCTION(Client) = opposite. UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) = all.
questions.correctAnswer: Marks a function as Server RPC — called on client, executes on server. UFUNCTION(Client) = opposite. UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) = all.
questions.explanation: Server RPC: client→server. Client RPC: server→owning client. NetMulticast: server→all clients. Must have _Implementation suffix on definition.

questions.q: What does UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable) do?
o: Client calls → executes on server. Reliable = guaranteed delivery in order. Must have _Implementation suffix.
questions.a: Client calls → executes on server. Reliable = guaranteed delivery in order. Must have _Implementation suffix.
questions.e: Server RPC: declared in header with UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable). Implementation: MyFunc_Implementation(). Client calls MyFunc() → server executes _Implementation.

questions.q: What does UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) do?
o: Server calls → executes on server AND all clients. Used for effects everyone should see.
questions.a: Server calls → executes on server AND all clients. Used for effects everyone should see.
questions.e: NetMulticast: server invokes, all clients execute. No _Implementation suffix (called directly). Use for explosions, announcements, global effects.

questions.q: What is the difference between Reliable and Unreliable RPCs?
o: Reliable: guaranteed delivery, ordered, can bottleneck. Unreliable: may drop, no guarantee, faster. Use Unreliable for frequent/fire-and-forget.
questions.a: Reliable: guaranteed delivery, ordered, can bottleneck. Unreliable: may drop, no guarantee, faster. Use Unreliable for frequent/fire-and-forget.
questions.e: Reliable = TCP-like. Unreliable = UDP-like. Use Reliable for critical events (damage, death). Use Unreliable for cosmetic (footsteps, muzzle flash).

questions.q: What does WithValidation add to an RPC?
o: Adds _Validate function — server checks parameters before executing. Returns false to block. Anti-cheat measure.
questions.a: Adds _Validate function — server checks parameters before executing. Returns false to block. Anti-cheat measure.
questions.e: UFUNCTION(Server, WithValidation). Server calls MyFunc_Validate(params) → if false, kicks client. Validates damage values, ability usage, movement limits.

questions.question: What does UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Health) do?
options: When Health replicates from server to client, OnRep_Health() callback is called on client — add effects, UI updates
questions.correctAnswer: When Health replicates from server to client, OnRep_Health() callback is called on client — add effects, UI updates
questions.explanation: ReplicatedUsing enables callback on value change. Used for reactive effects: damage flash, health bar update. OnRep receives the OLD value for comparison.

questions.q: What does UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Func) trigger?
o: When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI
questions.a: When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI
questions.e: ReplicatedUsing: client receives new value → OnRep callback fires. Used for reactive UI, damage effects, state transitions.

questions.q: What is the difference between Replicated and ReplicatedUsing?
o: Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.
questions.a: Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.
questions.e: Use Replicated for position/velocity (handled by movement component). Use ReplicatedUsing for health (need damage flash), state (need animation trigger).

questions.q: Does the server call OnRep when it changes a ReplicatedUsing variable?
o: No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.
questions.a: No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.
questions.e: OnRep is client-only. Server: change variable → call effect function manually OR rely on the client to trigger effects via OnRep.

questions.q: What is PushModel (UE5) for replication?
o: Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.
questions.a: Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.
questions.e: PushModel: opt-in. You explicitly mark properties dirty. Without it, UE compares ALL replicated properties every tick (expensive). PushModel reduces comparison cost.

questions.question: What is TArray's growth strategy?
options: Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
questions.correctAnswer: Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
questions.explanation: TArray = dynamic array. Similar to vector. Grow by adding slack (default 4 elements at small sizes). Reserve() prevents reallocation.

questions.q: What does TArray::Emplace do vs Add?
o: Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.
questions.a: Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.
questions.e: Emplace like emplace_back. Avoids temporary creation. TArray::Emplace(Args...) forwards to element constructor at new end position.

questions.q: What does TArray::Reserve do?
o: Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds
questions.a: Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds
questions.e: Reserve(N) = allocate N slots upfront. Push N elements without reallocation. Same as std::vector::reserve.

questions.q: What is TArray's growth strategy when full?
o: Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.
questions.a: Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.
questions.e: UE adds Slack (default=4) each growth. Also grows by old_capacity. Larger arrays grow faster. Amortized O(1) Add like std::vector.

questions.q: What does TArray::SetNum do?
o: Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess
questions.a: Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess
questions.e: SetNum(N) = resize to N. If N > size: default-construct new elements. If N < size: destroy excess elements. Does NOT change capacity.

questions.question: What data structure does TMap use?
options: Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
questions.correctAnswer: Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
questions.explanation: TMap = unordered hash map. Uses sparse array with chaining. Key must have GetTypeHash(). No ordering guarantees. O(1) average lookup.

questions.q: What does TMap::Find do?
o: Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.
questions.a: Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.
questions.e: FVector* val = Map.Find(key); if(val) use(*val); Standard UE pattern — pointer-based lookup, no exceptions.

questions.q: What hash function does TMap require?
o: GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.
questions.a: GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.
questions.e: TMap requires GetTypeHash(const T&). Built-in types, FString, FName have it. Custom structs: add friend uint32 GetTypeHash(const FMyStruct&).

questions.q: What does TMap::FindOrAdd do?
o: Returns reference to value for key, creating default-constructed value if key doesn't exist
questions.a: Returns reference to value for key, creating default-constructed value if key doesn't exist
questions.e: FindOrAdd: returns existing value or creates new entry. Useful for counters, caches. TMap[Key] = TMap::FindOrAdd(Key) + 1 style patterns.

questions.q: How is TMap different from TMultiMap?
o: TMap: one value per key (unique). TMultiMap: multiple values per key (duplicate keys allowed).
questions.a: TMap: one value per key (unique). TMultiMap: multiple values per key (duplicate keys allowed).
questions.e: TMap: unique keys. TMultiMap: multiple entries with same key. TMultiMap::Find returns first match. Use for key→many relationships.

questions.question: What is O(1) and when is it achievable?
options: Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
questions.correctAnswer: Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
questions.explanation: O(1) = runtime doesn't grow with input. arr[i], map.find(key) average. The holy grail. Rare for complex operations.

questions.question: What is O(n²) and why is it dangerous?
options: Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
questions.correctAnswer: Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
questions.explanation: O(n²): bubble sort, naive collision detection. Double the input = 4x the time. Must be avoided at scale. Use hashing or spatial partitioning.

questions.q: What is O(log n) and what algorithms achieve it?
o: Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.
questions.a: Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.
questions.e: log₂(1M) ≈ 20. Binary search, BST find/insert, heap push/pop. Any 'divide problem size by constant each step' algorithm.

questions.q: What is O(n log n) and why is it the lower bound for comparison sorting?
o: Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.
It could be faster with quantum computing
questions.a: Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.
questions.e: Ω(n log n) = lower bound for comparison-based sorting. Merge sort, heap sort, quicksort (average) achieve this. Counting sort beats it by NOT comparing.

questions.q: What is amortized O(1) and how does vector::push_back achieve it?
o: Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.
questions.a: Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.
questions.e: Amortized = average over sequence. Push N elements: O(N) total = O(1) amortized per push. Reallocation copies old elements — costs O(old_size).

questions.question: What does the dot product a·b compute geometrically?
options: |a||b|cos(θ) — the projection of a onto b, times b's length. Measures how parallel they are.
questions.correctAnswer: |a||b|cos(θ) — the projection of a onto b, times b's length. Measures how parallel they are.
questions.explanation: a·b > 0 = acute angle. a·b = 0 = perpendicular. a·b < 0 = obtuse. Used everywhere: lighting (N·L), projection, angle measurement.

questions.question: What does the cross product a×b compute?
options: A vector perpendicular to both a and b, magnitude = |a||b|sin(θ) (area of parallelogram). Direction: right-hand rule.
questions.correctAnswer: A vector perpendicular to both a and b, magnitude = |a||b|sin(θ) (area of parallelogram). Direction: right-hand rule.
questions.explanation: Cross product: normal vector computation, torque, angular momentum. Only defined in 3D (and 7D). Wedge product generalizes to any dimension.

questions.question: What is the relationship between sin, cos and the unit circle?
options: (cos θ, sin θ) is a point on the unit circle at angle θ from the x-axis
questions.correctAnswer: (cos θ, sin θ) is a point on the unit circle at angle θ from the x-axis
questions.explanation: Unit circle: radius=1. cos = x-coordinate, sin = y-coordinate. cos² + sin² = 1 (Pythagorean identity). Foundation of all trigonometry.

questions.question: What are radians and why are they used?
options: Natural angle measure: 1 radian = arc length equals radius. 2π rad = 360°. Makes calculus clean (derivative of sin is cos).
questions.correctAnswer: Natural angle measure: 1 radian = arc length equals radius. 2π rad = 360°. Makes calculus clean (derivative of sin is cos).
questions.explanation: In radians: d/dx sin(x) = cos(x). In degrees: d/dx sin(x°) = (π/180)cos(x°). Radians make the math elegant. Game engines use both.

questions.q: What is vector normalization?
o: Dividing a vector by its magnitude to produce a unit vector (length=1) — preserves direction
questions.a: Dividing a vector by its magnitude to produce a unit vector (length=1) — preserves direction
questions.e: v̂ = v/|v|. Used for direction vectors, surface normals, lighting. Normalizing zero vector = undefined (avoid).

questions.question: What is UObject?
options: Base class for all UE objects — provides GC, serialization, reflection, networking. Everything inherits from UObject.
questions.correctAnswer: Base class for all UE objects — provides GC, serialization, reflection, networking. Everything inherits from UObject.
questions.explanation: UObject = UE's base. AActor, UActorComponent, UAsset all derive from UObject. GC tracking via GUObjectArray. UPROPERTY for GC references.

questions.q: What does UCLASS() macro generate?
o: UHT generates: StaticClass() function, class flags (EClassCastFlags), CDO (Class Default Object), reflection data
questions.a: UHT generates: StaticClass() function, class flags (EClassCastFlags), CDO (Class Default Object), reflection data
questions.e: UCLASS = UE's reflection. StaticClass() returns UClass*. Cast<T> uses class flags. CDO = template instance with default property values.

questions.q: What is the Class Default Object (CDO)?
o: The default instance of a UClass — created at class registration. Used as template: new objects copy CDO's property values.
questions.a: The default instance of a UClass — created at class registration. Used as template: new objects copy CDO's property values.
questions.e: CDO = prototype object. Created once per class at engine init. NewObject<T>() copies CDO's properties. Editor defaults = CDO properties.

questions.q: What is the UObject creation path?
o: NewObject<T>(): allocates memory, copies CDO properties, calls constructor, registers with GC, calls PostInitProperties
questions.a: NewObject<T>(): allocates memory, copies CDO properties, calls constructor, registers with GC, calls PostInitProperties
questions.e: NewObject = UE's object factory. Never use 'new' for UObjects. NewObject handles GC registration, CDO copy, property initialization.

questions.q: How does UE garbage collection know an object is reachable?
o: Mark-and-sweep: starts from root set (UGameInstance, UWorld), follows UPROPERTY references. Unmarked = unreachable = delete.
questions.a: Mark-and-sweep: starts from root set (UGameInstance, UWorld), follows UPROPERTY references. Unmarked = unreachable = delete.
questions.e: UE GC: mark phase (traverse UPROPERTY graph from roots) → sweep phase (delete unmarked). Raw pointers DON'T prevent GC — must be UPROPERTY.

questions.question: What does UHT (Unreal Header Tool) do?
options: Parses UCLASS/USTRUCT/UFUNCTION macros and generates reflection data — .generated.h files with StaticClass(), serialization, GC info
questions.correctAnswer: Parses UCLASS/USTRUCT/UFUNCTION macros and generates reflection data — .generated.h files with StaticClass(), serialization, GC info
questions.explanation: UHT pre-processes headers before the compiler. UCLASS→StaticClass(), class flags. UPROPERTY→serialization, replication. UFUNCTION→network RPC, Blueprint.

questions.q: What does UPROPERTY() do?
o: Marks member for GC tracking, serialization, replication, Blueprint access — UHT generates reflection code for marked properties
questions.a: Marks member for GC tracking, serialization, replication, Blueprint access — UHT generates reflection code for marked properties
questions.e: UPROPERTY = reflection + GC. Without it: no GC tracking (dangling!), no serialization (can't save/load), no Blueprint/network access.

questions.q: What does UFUNCTION() do?
o: Marks function for Blueprint call, network RPC, or exec command — UHT generates thunk code for cross-boundary calls
questions.a: Marks function for Blueprint call, network RPC, or exec command — UHT generates thunk code for cross-boundary calls
questions.e: UFUNCTION enables BlueprintCallable, Server/Client/NetMulticast RPCs, Exec (console). UHT generates thunks that marshal parameters.

questions.q: What is the difference between BlueprintCallable and BlueprintPure?
o: Callable: has execution pin (can modify state). Pure: no exec pin (can't modify), evaluated on demand like a getter.
questions.a: Callable: has execution pin (can modify state). Pure: no exec pin (can't modify), evaluated on demand like a getter.
questions.e: BlueprintCallable = void/impure function (white exec pins). BlueprintPure = const function (green, no pins). Use Pure for getters, math.

questions.q: What are the UFUNCTION specifiers for networking?
questions.correctAnswer: Server, Client, NetMulticast, Reliable, Unreliable, WithValidation
questions.explanation: Server: client→server. Client: server→owning client. NetMulticast: server→all. Reliable: guaranteed delivery. WithValidation: adds _Validate check.
